entry.title: How We Built ASTRA Joule Agent for Automation Testing Solution for SAP SuccessFactors HCM
entry.content:
Introduction

In my experience working on multiple SAP SuccessFactors HCM implementations, one phase that consistently stands out as challenging is testing. No matter how well the system is designed, testing often becomes time-consuming and difficult to manage—especially when dealing with multiple modules like Employee Central, Payroll, and integrations.

During several projects, I observed that teams were spending a significant amount of effort on repetitive manual validation, coordination across systems, and analyzing failures. While automation tools helped to some extent, they still required heavy setup and maintenance.

This led us to explore how we could make testing more efficient and intelligent using SAP BTP and AI capabilities. That’s how we started working on what we now call the ASTRA Joule HR Agent—a solution aimed at simplifying and improving the testing experience.

The Challenge We Faced

From a practical standpoint, testing in SAP SuccessFactors is not just about executing scripts—it involves validating complete business processes and ensuring data consistency across systems.

Some of the common challenges we encountered were:

  • Handling large regression cycles with hundreds of scenarios
  • Managing complex and realistic test data
  • Difficulty in identifying the root cause of failures
  • Constant switching between multiple tools (SuccessFactors, test tools, Jira, etc.)

These issues often slowed down release cycles and required significant manual effort.

Our Approach – ASTRA Joule HR Agent

To address these gaps, we built ASTRA as a centralized, intelligent powerhouse on SAP Business Technology Platform. We leveraged SAP Business AI through Joule skills to create a conversational, "human-centric" testing experience.

Instead of navigating complex menus, users can interact with ASTRA through SAP Build Work Zone. By integrating Joule, we’ve made it possible to manage tests, gain insights into failures, and even trigger executions using natural language.

Key Technical Highlights:

  • Prebuilt Library: We launched with 926+ automation scripts aligned with SAP Best Practices.

  • Full-Stack Integration: Built using the SAP Cloud Application Programming  model, ensuring seamless OData connectivity with SuccessFactors and S/4HANA.

  • AI Orchestration: Using Joule to provide smarter interactions and faster insights into test logs.

Benefits

1. Extensive Module Coverage

ASTRA doesn’t just "test"; it validates the entire HCM lifecycle. This includes Employee Central, Recruiting, Onboarding, Benefits, Time Management, and Performance & Goals. It even handles custom Extension Center objects, ensuring your unique business logic is never left unchecked.

 

 

2. End-to-End & Integration Testing - Enables both functional and integration testing across SAP SuccessFactors, SAP ERP, SAP S/4HANA, and external systems—helping ensure full end-to-end process validation.

 

 

 

 

 

 

3. AI-Powered Insights (Joule Skills)

By utilizing SAP Business AI, ASTRA helps users understand why a test failed without digging through raw logs. Joule assists in identifying failed scenarios and provides intelligent summaries, drastically reducing investigation time.

 

 

 

 

4. Dynamic, Input-Driven Execution

Efficiency is key. ASTRA allows users to enter specific Entity IDs to run focused tests for individual records. The system dynamically filters and executes only the relevant test cases based on your input data.

 

 

 

5. Automated Documentation & Defect Management

  • Traceability: Every execution generates a PDF-based result document for step-by-step audit trails.

  • Jira Integration: Failed scenarios automatically trigger ticket creation in JIRA, ensuring the engineering team is notified in real-time.

 

 

 

6. Joule-Powered Assistance with SAP Business AI (Joule Skills)

  • Uses SAP Business AI through Joule skills to enhance test execution and analysis.
  • Provides intelligent assistance to help users understand test results and failures faster.
  • Helps identify failed scenarios and related logs without manual analysis.
  • Improves overall testing efficiency by reducing time spent on investigation and coordination.

 

 

Business Value

From what I’ve observed while working with this approach, the impact is quite practical and noticeable in day-to-day project execution:

  • Faster Release Cycles: With better visibility into test results and fewer manual dependencies, we were able to move through release cycles with more confidence and less delay.
  • Reduced Manual Effort: A significant portion of repetitive validation was minimized, which allowed both functional and technical teams to spend more time on critical tasks rather than regression testing.
  • Improved Data Reliability: By validating end-to-end scenarios with realistic test data, we could identify issues earlier and avoid unexpected problems during go-live.
  • Better Visibility for Stakeholders: Having centralized dashboards within SAP Build Work Zone made it easier for stakeholders to track progress, understand results, and make informed decisions without relying on multiple tools.

Conclusion

Based on my experience, testing in SAP SuccessFactors projects often becomes a coordination-heavy activity. What worked well for us with this approach was shifting the focus from managing test execution to actually improving system quality.

By leveraging capabilities available on SAP Business Technology Platform along with Joule, we were able to make testing more streamlined and easier to manage.

The ASTRA Joule HR Agent, as we implemented it, is not just about automation—it’s about making testing more meaningful, reducing effort, and helping teams deliver stable and reliable HR solutions more consistently.

entry.title: Introducing ATUL Architecture : A New Blog Series on SAP BTP for Intelligent Utilities
entry.content:
Over the past few years, I’ve had the chance to work closely with utilities that are modernizing their operations using SAP S/4HANA, SAP BTP, and event‑driven, real‑time architectures. One thing has become very clear: Utilities need simple, practical architecture patterns — not product brochures, not theory, and not marketing slides.

So I’m starting a new blog series called ATUL Architecture, where I’ll share practical patterns, diagrams, and lessons learned from real projects. The goal is simple: Make SAP BTP architecture easier to understand and easier to apply in the utilities industry.

This series is for architects, consultants, developers, and anyone working on SAP‑based utility transformation.

What the Series Will Cover

Here’s the plan for the first six posts:

ATUL Architecture #1 — Why Utilities Need a Digital Core on SAP BTP

  • Clean core

  • Integration strategy

  • Why BTP becomes the “digital backbone”

  • A simple digital‑core diagram

This post sets the foundation for the entire series.

 

ATUL Architecture #2 — Event‑Driven Utilities: Designing Real‑Time Flows on SAP BTP

  • Event Mesh

  • AMI → BTP → S/4HANA flow

  • Real‑time vs batch

  • A simple event‑flow diagram

This will focus on how utilities can move from slow batch processes to real‑time operations.

 

ATUL Architecture #3 — Bridging OT and IT: Bringing Grid Data into SAP BTP Safely

  • IEC 61850

  • MQTT

  • Substation → BTP patterns

  • Security considerations

This post will show how OT data can be brought into SAP BTP in a safe and scalable way.

 

ATUL Architecture #4 — AI on SAP BTP for Grid Operations

  • Time‑series data

  • Anomaly detection

  • Predictive maintenance

  • AI → workflow → S/4HANA integration

This will focus on how AI becomes actionable inside SAP processes.

 

ATUL Architecture #5 — Designing a Utility Data Fabric on SAP BTP

  • Data lake

  • Semantic models

  • Governance

  • Analytics for grid + customer

This post will cover how utilities can build a unified data layer on BTP.

 

ATUL Architecture #6 — Intelligent Utility Blueprint: End‑to‑End Architecture on SAP BTP

  • One consolidated diagram

  • End‑to‑end flow

  • Real‑time + AI + automation

  • Business outcomes

This will be the “big picture” post — the one that ties everything together.

I Want Your Input

Before I publish the first post, I’d love to hear from the SAP Community:

  • What topics would you like to see in this series?

  • Are there specific SAP BTP patterns you want explained?

  • Any challenges you’re facing in utilities, integration, or real‑time operations?

  • Should I include more diagrams, code snippets, or architecture trade‑offs?

Your suggestions will help shape the series.

What’s Next

I’ll publish ATUL Architecture #1 — Why Utilities Need a Digital Core on SAP BTP next. It will include a simple diagram and a practical explanation of why BTP becomes the digital backbone for modern utilities.

Looking forward to your ideas and feedback.

entry.title: New Dates for Get Certified: SAP BTP Administrator Live Sessions
entry.content:
Since TechEd last year, the certification for SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) Administrator is available. It is a system-based assessment, where you complete tasks in an authentic SAP Software environment – a real SAP BTP global account.

Currently, I am delivering the second time the Get Certified: SAP BTP Administrator live session track, which introduces you to this new format of hands-on assessments and summarizes the topics covered in the certification.

Because we are seeing a high demand for these live sessions, we just added additional dates for you in July, September, and November in 2026. Please join me in the journey to become an SAP BTP Administrator.

💡Hint:
The core of the Administrating SAP Business Technology Platform learning journey leading to the certification is the Operating SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP200) course. Check-out my blog post summarizing BTP200.

entry.title: New Course Available: Mastering SAP Customer Identity and Access Management
entry.content:
We’re pleased to announce the availability of a new course on SAP Learning:
Mastering SAP Customer Identity and Access Management.

👉Access the course here:
Mastering SAP Customer Identity and Access Management

This standalone course is designed to help professionals build strong, practical skills in configuring and managing SAP Customer Data Cloud for modern, compliant, and seamless customer identity experiences.

Why This Course?

Customer identity is at the core of trusted digital experiences. Organisations today must securely manage customer data, comply with evolving regulations, and support seamless interactions across channels and devices—all while enabling personalisation at scale.

Mastering SAP Customer Identity and Access Management provides a comprehensive introduction to SAP Customer Data Cloud, focusing on real‑world configuration and administration tasks that directly support customer identity and access management (CIAM) scenarios.

Learning Outcome

By the end of this course, learners will be able to configure and administer SAP Customer Data Cloud to support secure and compliant customer identity management. Learners will design and manage account and data store schemas, including group models; configure site settings using the SAP Customer Data Console; and implement lite and full registration flows, authentication options, and identity journeys using UI Builder, while applying security and governance best practices.

Course Structure

The course is structured into logical units that guide learners from platform context through hands‑on configuration:

  1. SAP Customer Data Cloud in the Modern CX and Regulatory Landscape
    Understand how SAP Customer Data Cloud supports regulatory compliance and how it fits within the SAP Customer Experience portfolio.

  2. Console Site Configuration and Administration
    Learn how to navigate the SAP Customer Data Console, configure essential site settings, and work with customer data.

  3. Designing and Managing Data Schemas
    Design and manage account and data store schemas, edit schema fields, configure advanced data stores, and create group models for organizing customer accounts.

  4. Managing Customer Identity and Access
    Implement lite and full registration flows, manage identity journeys using UI Builder, and configure authentication and security options.

This structure ensures a clear progression from foundational concepts to practical identity and access management scenarios.

Who Is This Course For?

This intermediate‑level course is designed for professionals who work with SAP Customer Data Cloud and customer identity solutions, including:

  • SAP Customer Data Cloud administrators
  • Implementation consultants
  • CX professionals involved in customer identity, registration, and data management

Developer‑specific topics are addressed separately in other courses within the broader CIAM curriculum.

Part of a Growing CIAM Curriculum

This course is the first of four CIAM‑focused courses that will be added to the Learning Journey Implementing SAP Customer Data Cloud on learning.sap.com. Together, these courses will progressively expand the available self‑paced learning content and provide comprehensive coverage of SAP Customer Data Cloud capabilities.

Get Started

Whether you’re implementing SAP Customer Data Cloud for the first time or strengthening your existing expertise, Mastering SAP Customer Identity and Access Management is the ideal place to start.

👉Enroll now:
Mastering SAP Customer Identity and Access Management

Stay tuned—more CIAM courses are coming soon.

entry.title: SAP DMS Client – A Desktop Companion for Cloud Integration Data Archiving
entry.content:
Why I Built This

While looking into Cloud Integration data archiving, I came across Kishor Gopinathan's blog post SAP Cloud Integration – Data Archiving to BTP SDM. It walks through the full setup — creating service instances, building the CloudIntegration_LogArchive destination, and activating archiving via API.

I followed it while onboarding onto a new Integration Suite landscape, and it worked great. But I noticed that the workflow assumes we have a REST client like Postman at hand to call APIs, and some familiarity with CMIS to browse the archived content. Not everyone on the team has that setup ready — and honestly, assembling JSON payloads and fetching OAuth tokens just to activate archiving felt like unnecessary friction for what should be a straightforward task.

So I built a small desktop app to lower that barrier: the SAP DMS Client. I built it with the help of AI tooling, which made it realistic to go from idea to working desktop app within my onboarding timeline. It reads our DMS and Cloud Integration service keys and gives us a UI for managing repositories, exporting destination files, activating archiving, and browsing archived content — no REST client or CMIS knowledge required. The code is open source, so anyone can inspect and adapt it.

What It Does

The app is built with Electron, React, and Material UI (following SAP Horizon design guidelines). Once we load our two service key files — one for DMS, one for Process Integration Runtime — we get four things:

  • Repository management — create, list, and delete DMS repositories without CMIS API calls
  • Destination export — generate a CloudIntegration_LogArchive.json with all credentials pre-filled, ready to import into BTP Cockpit
  • Archiving activation — activate Cloud Integration data archiving in one click (the app handles the OAuth2 token exchange)
  • File explorer — browse the YYYY/MM/DD folder structure that Cloud Integration creates in the repository

The destination export is probably the biggest time saver. Instead of manually extracting clientId, clientSecret, tokenServiceURL, and the SDM endpoint from the service key JSON, the app assembles the full destination file automatically. We just import it into BTP Cockpit and move on.

Here's what the generated file looks like:

{
  "Name": "CloudIntegration_LogArchive",
  "Type": "HTTP",
  "URL": "https://<sdm-endpoint>/browser",
  "Authentication": "OAuth2ClientCredentials",
  "RepositoryId": "<repository-id>",
  "clientId": "<from-service-key>",
  "clientSecret": "<from-service-key>",
  "tokenServiceURL": "https://<auth-endpoint>/oauth/token"
}

No copy-paste errors, no missed fields.

One thing to note: when we import the destination into BTP Cockpit, all fields are populated automatically except the client secret — BTP doesn't include it in the import format for security reasons. We'll need to grab the clientSecret from our DMS service key file and paste it manually into the destination configuration in the Cockpit after import.

What We Need Before Starting

The same BTP services from the original blog:

Service Plan What We Need

Integration Suiteenterprise / standardCloud Integration capability activated
Process Integration RuntimeapiService instance + service key
Document Management Service, Integration OptionstandardService instance + service key

Setup details: Initial Setup for DMS and Creating Service Key for Cloud Integration.

The app is on GitHub at github.com/sap-ef/da-dms-client. The easiest way to get started is to download a pre-built installer from the Releases page:

  • macOS — .dmg file
  • Windows — .exe installer

Alternatively, we can build from source with Node.js 18+:

git clone https://github.com/sap-ef/da-dms-client.git
cd da-dms-client
npm install
npm run electron:dev

The Workflow in Practice

Once the app is running, the flow follows a specific order — each step depends on the previous one:

  1. Load the DMS service key in Settings — this connects us to our Document Management Service instance
  2. Create a repository — we need a repository before we can generate a destination (the destination includes the repository ID)
  3. Export the destination file — the app generates the CloudIntegration_LogArchive.json with all the values from the service key
  4. Import the destination into the Integration Suite subaccount — in BTP Cockpit under Connectivity > Destinations (remember to fill in the client secret manually after import)
  5. Load the Cloud Integration service key in Settings — this connects us to the Process Integration Runtime instance
  6. Activate data archiving — this only works if the destination already exists in BTP, otherwise Cloud Integration won't know where to write the logs

 

Don't expect logs to appear immediately. As described in the original blog, the log collection process runs on a 7-day cycle — so it may take several days before the first archived files show up in the repository.

After archiving is active, the File Explorer lets us browse what Cloud Integration has stored. The folder structure is date-based (2026/04/15/) with .zip archives of message processing logs for each day.

Conclusion

Kishor Gopinathan's original blog remains the foundation for understanding Cloud Integration data archiving to SDM. The DMS Client just makes the repetitive parts easier so we can spend less time on plumbing and more on the integration itself.

The project is open source at github.com/sap-ef/da-dms-client. If you've dealt with Cloud Integration archiving in your landscape, I'd be curious to hear how you've approached it — or if there are other parts of the DMS workflow that could use better tooling. Drop a comment or open an issue.

Links

  • SAP DMS Client — GitHub
  • SAP Cloud Integration – Data Archiving to BTP SDM — Kishor Gopinathan
  • Initial Setup for Document Management Service
  • Creating Service Instance and Service Key (Cloud Integration)

entry.title: Something Exciting is on the Horizon: Your Sapphire 2026 Session Guide
entry.content:
If you have not already, check out Mark Wright's blog post on What to Expect from AI Agents and Agentic Development — it sets the stage perfectly for what is coming.

Sapphire 2026  is shaping up to be one of the most exciting yet. This year, we will be unveiling a wave of new AI features that our team has been developing behind the scenes. We cannot wait to share them with you.

Whether you are just getting started with SAP Build and Joule Studio or you are already an expert, there is something here for you. Our sessions are designed to meet you where you are — giving you the insights, demos, and hands-on learning to take your skills to the next level.

Join us at Sapphire 2026 — register for the format that works best for you:

  • SAP Sapphire & ASUG Annual Conference Orlando
  • SAP Sapphire Virtual
  • SAP Sapphire Madrid

 

If you are planning to visit SAP Sapphire in person, stop by our live event demo booth to meet the team and see innovation in action. Discover how SAP Build helps teams create AI-powered solutions—from extensions to workflows and agentic apps—so you can deliver faster with more agility and less complexity. Do not miss our Agent Lab experience, where you can take part in a hands-on session and see just how easy it is to build AI agents that streamline and simplify your daily work:

  • Orlando station (BTP2301) and Agent Lab Experience Hub (EXP2788)
  • Madrid station (BTP1408) and Agent Lab Experience Hub (EXP1537)

 

To help you plan your agenda, we have also curated a selection of must-attend sessions. These highlight the latest AI capabilities for agent-based applications and workflow development, bringing them to life through real-world examples.

One session, however, stands out as essential: “The Enterprise Path to Agentic AI” (BTP1454). Presented by Michael Gall, Sid Misra, and Corey Swenson, this session explores how Joule Studio enables the creation of AI solutions ranging from simple extensions to fully agentic applications. It also highlights SAP’s strategy for delivering an enterprise-grade, intent-to-execution platform tailored for SAP customers. Attendees will see how the Joule solution provides a unified, guided experience — leading developers from initial intent through blueprinting and agent specification to testing and deployment.

entry.title: Product-Agent Fit: A Practical Framework for Building on SAP BTP in the Age of Joule
entry.content:
A practical framework for SAP architects, developers, and product managers navigating the agentic enterprise

The Question SAP Is Already Asking

At SAP TechEd 2025 in Berlin, SAP Executive Board Member Muhammad Alam, in charge of SAP Product & Engineering, said SAP was entering “the era of agentic AI, where AI moves from being just a tool to becoming your trusted teammate."

A few months later, on March 31, 2026, SAP CEO Christian Klein went further in his blog: "Software is becoming the operating system for trusted autonomy." (Klein, March: “Software is Entering Its Most Powerful Era”)

These are not marketing statements. They signal architectural direction — and they have direct implications for every SAP developer, consultant, and product manager building on BTP today.

Here is the question they force us to ask about every application we build or extend:

Not only "will a user love this?" — but also "will Joule reach for it?"

That stretch — from human satisfaction to agent invocability — is what I want to explore in this post through a framework I have been developing: Product-Agent Fit (PAF).

 

What Is Product-Agent Fit?

For nearly two decades, the dominant lens for evaluating software products has been Product-Market Fit (PMF) — the degree to which a product satisfies a human market's needs well enough that users adopt it, pay for it, and recommend it. PMF assumes the validator of product value is always a human being with preferences, emotions, and tolerance for rough edges.

Product-Agent Fit (PAF) proposes an additional validator: the AI agent.

PAF is the degree to which a product's capability surface is naturally consumable by AI agents — such that agents discover it autonomously, invoke it correctly, trust its outputs, chain it into workflows, and operate within enterprise governance constraints — without human mediation at invocation time.

In SAP terms: does your CAP application, your Fiori app, your S/4HANA extension — does it work when Joule calls it? Not when a user clicks through it. When an agent invokes it.

The answer to that question is increasingly one of the most important singlas you would know about the long-term relevance of whatever you are building.

 

Why This Matters Specifically on SAP BTP

SAP has been executing a clear, multi-year trajectory toward agent-first architecture:

  • September 2023: Joule launched as a copilot — human-facing, UI-adjacent
  • October 2024: Joule went agentic — multi-agent orchestration, coordinating "across business functions" (TechEd 2024)
  • April 2025: SAP joined Google as a founding industry contributor to the A2A (Agent-to-Agent) protocol — standardizing how agents communicate across enterprise platforms
  • November 2025: SAP unveiled major advancements in agentic AI, including introducing new Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers at TechEd 2025, enabling Claude Code, Cursor, Cline, and Windsurf to access SAP development frameworks directly
  • March 2026: Christian Klein: "Building an agent is becoming increasingly easier—the tip of the iceberg. Deploying it across end-to-end supply chains or financial close processes, with full compliance and audit trails, is where most of the effort lies."

The direction is unambiguous. SAP is rebuilding its platform so that Joule — and agents beyond Joule — can consume every capability without depending on screen navigation.

The question for us as builders is: are the things we build on BTP keeping pace with that direction?

 

The PAF Framework — Mapped to SAP

I propose eleven dimensions for evaluating whether an SAP BTP application has achieved Product-Agent Fit. Each maps to specific SAP tools and patterns you can act on today.

Two SAP resources serve as the primary references throughout this framework. For agent-layer implementation — Joule Studio, MCP tooling, agent architecture, and the AI development lifecycle — SAP's AI Golden Path (architecture.learning.sap.com/docs/aigp) is the dedicated guide. For platform security, CAP patterns, ORD, and audit logging — the SAP BTP Developer's Guide applies. Where a dimension touches both layers, both are called out.

1. Capability Fit

Are your capabilities atomic enough for an agent to invoke directly?

In SAP terms: your OData V4 services, your CAP action handlers, your ABAP logic exposed as OData V4 services via RAP (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model) — are they scoped at the level of a single, invocable agent action? Not "manage the purchase order" but release_purchase_order(po_id, approver_id).

The 2,100+ pre-delivered Joule skills SAP has published by the end of 2025 are each scoped exactly this way. If you are building custom extensions, your capability granularity should match that standard.

SAP tool: CAP action definitions, OData V4 services built on RAP

2. Interface Fit

Is your API surface natively machine-readable without human explanation?

This is where MCP comes in. At TechEd 2025, SAP announced that developers using agentic coding tools can now access SAP development frameworks via SAP Build local MCP Servers. The open-source SAP MDK MCP Server (github.com/SAP/mdk-mcp-server) exposes Mobile Development Kit tools as MCP tools consumable by authorized MCP-compatible agents.

For your custom BTP application, Interface Fit means: is there a machine-readable, typed schema that describes what your service does, what parameters it takes, and what errors it returns — in a form an LLM can reason over without a human explaining it?

SAP tools: MCP Server definitions, OData V4 with typed EDM schemas, SAP AI SDK (Javascript: github.com/SAP/ai-sdk-js; Java: github.com/SAP/ai-sdk-java)

3. Reliability Fit

Are your outputs deterministic enough for agents to chain without verification overhead?

Christian Klein stated this precisely in his March 2026 blog post: "orchestration, policy enforcement, and workflow determinism are the gatekeepers of trust."

In practice, this means your business logic — especially anything involving financial calculations, inventory, or compliance — must be deterministic and auditable. Joule should call your service and trust the result. It should not need to re-verify the output or ask a human to confirm before passing it to the next step.

This is the separation-of-concerns principle that CAP embodies, now applied to agent consumption: your service layer handles determinism; Joule handles the reasoning. Do not mix these responsibilities.

SAP tool: CAP handlers with deterministic business logic, CDS-defined validation rules

4. Context Fit

Can Joule determine when and how to invoke your service from its schema alone?

SAP's Knowledge Graph (announced TechEd 2024) maps "relationships and context across SAP's vast data landscape" using fifty years of business process expertise — so Joule knows the semantics of your business domain, not just the syntax of your API.

For your custom application, this means: tool descriptions must be semantically rich enough for an LLM to correctly select your tool from a catalog of hundreds. A tool named processMaterial with no description will not be selected. A tool named check_material_availability with the description "Returns current stock level and ATP date for a given material in a specified plant" will be.

SAP tools: MCP tool descriptions, Joule Studio skill definitions, CDS annotations

5. Composability Fit

Does your service output feed directly into the next Joule step without transformation?

SAP's industry partnership in the A2A protocol (April 2025) is a direct investment in this dimension. A2A is intended to enable Joule to coordinate with agents from Google, Microsoft, and other vendors — "sharing context and acting together across enterprise platforms without requiring manual system switching or data loss."

For your application: every service response should be structured JSON that the next tool in the chain can consume directly. If your response is only a formatted HTML string or a PDF attachment, Joule cannot chain it — a human must intervene. That is a composability failure.

SAP tools: Structured CDS projections, A2A-compliant agent registration, Joule Studio orchestration

6. Headless Deployment Fit

Does your application deliver full value without a human opening a browser?

SAP repositioned Joule from AI copilot to "AI orchestrator across your entire organization" (February 2025) — meaning Joule should be able to execute complete workflows end-to-end, not just assist a human who is already on a screen.

Ask this about your application: if I remove the Fiori UI entirely, can Joule still accomplish the core business task? If the answer is no — because some step requires a human to click Confirm in the UI — that is a Headless Deployment Fit gap.

The fix is not to remove the UI. It is to ensure that human confirmation steps are exposed as structured approval actions that agents can surface through conversation, not as UI buttons that block the workflow.

SAP tools: CAP action handlers callable by MCP, Joule-invocable approval flows on SAP Build Work Zone

7. Trust Pipeline Fit

Does your application enforce governance at the API layer, not just the UI layer?

Walter Sun, SVP and Global Head of AI at SAP, has been direct: "accountability must always rest with humans or legal entities, never with the AI itself" (Sun, June 2025: “From Assistive AI to Agentic AI: Risks, Responsibilities, and the Road Ahead”). And Christian Klein identifies "validation rules, compliance checks, approval flows, identity management and audit trails" as non-negotiable requirements for enterprise agents (Klein, March 2026).

In practice, this means authorization enforcement must happen at your CAP service layer — not just at the Fiori UI. For new applications, this applies whether you are using XSUAA or the newer SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) and Authorization Management Service (AMS) stack that the SAP BTP Developer's Guide now recommends; the governance requirement is identical under both. An agent that bypasses the UI must encounter the same authorization gates as a human user. Every agent-invoked write should be logged to the SAP Audit Log Service with full provenance.

If your authorization logic lives only in the Fiori app and your CAP services are essentially open to any authenticated caller, you have a Trust Pipeline Fit gap — and a serious security risk in an agentic world.

SAP tools: Authorization enforcement in CAP handlers, SAP Audit Log Service, SAP Authorization and Trust Management

8. Longitudinal Context Fit

Does your application give Joule access to accumulated business knowledge, not just current-state data?

SAP's Business Data Cloud creates "a single trusted data layer" across SAP and non-SAP sources. The SAP Knowledge Graph leverages "50-plus years of business process expertise" to give agents semantic understanding of business relationships that raw data cannot provide.

For your custom application: does Joule know that this supplier has had three late deliveries in the past six months? Does it know that this customer's contract expires in 30 days? Does it understand the regulatory history of this tax position? If that context is locked in a data warehouse that Joule cannot query, your application cannot achieve longitudinal context fit.

SAP tools: SAP Business Data Cloud, SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine (for RAG), SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph Engine, SAP Analytics Cloud, CDS-defined analytical views

9. Surface-Agnostic Rendering Fit

Does your application reach the human wherever they are, not only inside Fiori?

Joule has been extended beyond SAP applications into third-party applications via WalkMe integration (SAP Sapphire, 2025), and Slack adoption for SAP agent interactions has grown significantly. The underlying principle is the same one Salesforce articulates for headless commerce and CRM — that the engagement surface can change (mobile app, Slack, Teams, voice interface) while the underlying business platform stays consistent: "The surface changes. The platform doesn't."

For your application: can the agent surface your workflow outcomes as a Slack notification, a Teams card, a mobile push alert — without you having to build a separate integration for each? If your application only surfaces results in Fiori, you are missing the engagement surfaces where your users actually spend their time.

SAP tools: SAP Build Work Zone, Joule in Slack integration, SAP Mobile Services, SAP BTP notification services

10. Agent Operational Maturity Fit

Can you detect and fix Joule behavioral drift without waiting for a user complaint?

SAP ships the LeanIX AI Agent Hub — dashboards showing agent deployment, process impact, and performance metrics. This is the SAP-side equivalent of what Salesforce calls its Testing Center and Observability tooling.

The critical insight from the industry: agents are probabilistic, not deterministic. There is no bug to fix — only behavior to observe and tune. If your Joule skill works correctly today but drifts after the next model update or business rule change, you need operational tooling to detect that drift before it affects production processes.

SAP tools: LeanIX AI Agent Hub, SAP AI Core evaluation service, Joule Studio agent testing

11. Marketplace Discovery Fit

Is your application discoverable by agents, not just by humans browsing the SAP Marketplace?

ORD — Open Resource Discovery is SAP's answer to this dimension. Now governed by the Linux Foundation Europe and NeoNephos Foundation (open-resource-discovery.org), ORD enables applications to "self-describe their exposed resources and capabilities" in a standardized, decentralized format. When your BTP application publishes ORD metadata, Joule can discover and compose your capabilities without a developer manually wiring the integration.

If your application is not publishing ORD metadata, it is invisible to the next generation of agent-based orchestration — regardless of how well it works when a human finds it manually.

SAP tools: ORD specification implementation in CAP, SAP Business Accelerator Hub publication, Joule Studio as a unified catalog and registration system 

 

The SAP PAF Maturity Ladder

Where does your application sit today?

Level 0 — INVISIBLE

  UI-only. No OData/CAP API. Joule cannot reach it.

  Example: Legacy Excel-based processes, classic Dynpro without API layer.

 

Level 1 — REACHABLE

  OData API exists but no ORD metadata, no MCP tools defined.

  Joule can be manually wired but won't discover it autonomously.

  Example: Standard CAP app without ORD publication.

 

Level 2 — CALLABLE

  OData V4 with typed schemas.

 Joule can invoke with explicit developer instruction. Errors are structured.

  Example: CAP app following the SAP BTP Developer's Guide, ORD partially configured.

 

Level 3 — DISCOVERABLE

  ORD metadata published. MCP tools defined in line with the SAP AI Golden Path.

  Joule Studio or other agent tooling can discover and wire the capability with much less manual effort.

  Example: Full SAP BTP Developer's Guide + SAP AI Golden Path + MCP + ORD.

 

Level 4 — PREFERRED

  Joule selects autonomously. Authorization enforced at CAP layer.

  Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) approval flows agent-accessible.

  Longitudinal context available via Business Data Cloud.

  Registered as A2A-compatible via SAP Agent Fabric / BTP agent runtime.

  Example: Production Joule Agent with full BTP governance.

 

Level 5 — FOUNDATIONAL

  Other Joule agents and third-party agents depend on yours as a building block.

  Discoverable within Joule Studio without manual wiring.

  Example: What SAP's own HANA Cloud and Master Data services are candidates to become.

 

What to Do This Sprint

With your application's level on the PAF Maturity Ladder identified, here is a concrete sprint-level action plan for each transition.

Moving from Level 1 to Level 2: Publish your CAP service with a typed OData V4 schema. Define structured error responses using CAP's error handling framework. Ensure every action has a meaningful, LLM-readable description in your CDS annotations.

Moving from Level 2 to Level 3: Implement ORD metadata in your CAP application. Define at least one MCP tool wrapping your most-used CAP action. SAP's AI Golden Path (architecture.learning.sap.com/docs/aigp) is the recommended starting point for MCP tool definitions, Joule Studio registration, and the agent development lifecycle. Test that Joule can invoke your tool without a human explaining the parameters, with structured errors and audit trace (in natural language).

Moving from Level 3 to Level 4: Move authorization enforcement into your CAP service handlers — not just Fiori app routing. Add Audit Log Service integration for every agent-invoked write. Design your human approval flows as CAP actions that return structured confirmation tokens, not (just) as UI buttons. Register your agent as A2A-compatible via SAP Agent Fabric or the BTP agent runtime to enable cross-vendor agent interoperability.

Moving from Level 4 to Level 5: Document your MCP tool definitions in a format LLMs can parse. Publish relevant APIs, packages, or reusable assets where appropriate. Consider submitting your Joule skill to the SAP partner ecosystem so other agent stacks can discover and reuse your capability.

 

The Framing That Changes How You Build

I want to close with Christian Klein's formulation, because I think it is the most important framing for every SAP practitioner today:

"Building an agent is becoming increasingly easier—the tip of the iceberg. Deploying it across end-to-end supply chains or financial close processes, with full compliance and audit trails, is where most of the effort lies."

That sentence captures the challenge precisely. The hard part is not building the agent. The hard part is making your platform ready for the agent — so that when Joule arrives at your CAP service, your OData endpoint, your approval workflow, it finds something it can invoke, trust, govern, and chain.

That readiness is Product-Agent Fit.

And based on the trajectory of SAP's own platform — A2A, MCP, ORD, Joule Studio, LeanIX AI Agent Hub — it is clear that SAP is building many PAF-like requirements into the platform layer. The question is whether the applications we build on top of that platform are keeping pace.

Over to You

I am curious where the SAP community currently stands on this:

  • Are you already designing your BTP applications with Joule invocability in mind, or is agent consumption still a future concern?
  • Has your team published ORD metadata or defined MCP tools yet?
  • Where do most of your current applications sit on the PAF Maturity Ladder?

Drop your thoughts in the comments. The more specific the better — I would love to hear concrete patterns, blockers, and solutions from those already building at the frontier.

entry.title: Blog 5 - Beyond Data Warehousing: AI-assisted migration of Business Logic from BW 7.5 to BDC Part 2
entry.content:
What if you could modernize your decades-old SAP BW investment without losing a single line of critical business logic? SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) makes this reality possible through AI-assisted migration capabilities that transform traditional BW modernization challenges into strategic opportunities. Organizations can now seamlessly transition from rigid Business Warehouse environments to agile, future-proof data ecosystems that deliver trusted, delta-enabled data products for fact-based decision support while preserving decades of critical business investments.

As a recap in the previous blogs, we have seen

  1. the creation of AI-ready Data Products from SAP BW via ‘Data Product Generator’, that replicates BW Infoprovider data and its semantics to SAP HANA Cloud Data Lake files Storage (HDLFS) within SAP BDC for analytical and AI/ML scenarios/workflows.
  2. how to integrate BW standard extractors and preserve custom extractors business logic into SAP BDC.
  3. how to leverage existing BW custom logic in ABAP transformations to replicate to SAP Business Data Cloud by using GenAI functionality in GenAI Hub and allowing human in the loop (HITL) to review and improve the AI-assisted generated SQL code.

You can find below the previous blogs, plus the links to the subsequent blogs will be listed here:

  • A.I Assisted Transition at your own Pace: BW7.5 to BDC Pilot Experiences
  • Blog 2 - Accelerating SAP BW Modernization: Hands on Experiences with the BDC Data Product Generator
  • Blog 3 - From BW 7.5 to SAP BDC: Reusing Extractors, Custom Logic, and Models Standard Extractors
  • Blog 4 - Beyond Data Warehousing: AI-assisted migration of Business Logic from BW 7.5 to BDC Part 1
  • Link5 – TO BE UPDATED

In this blog, the focus is on how SAP BW 7.5 on-premise customers can replicate and modernize their custom BW business logic as part of BW BEx Query, BW custom ABAP authorization and user exit variables into SAP BDC and leveraging SAP Datasphere, Business Technology Platform and Business Data Cloud capabilities, based on practical experiences in a recent pilot project.

Business Challenges and Objective

Most of the mature BW customers have built for many years complex custom business logic in their ad-hoc and operational reporting via SAP BW BEx Query design that apply only to their business needs while ensuring the right data access and control to the end users. In order to move to the cloud and adopt AI and innovation in data and analytics, they need to ensure seamless migration of their existing business logic and processes without manually re-designing the new environment.

In the example below, we will illustrate how a BW customer can replicate their custom BW business logic written in ABAP used in BW BEx Query as user exit variable into the BDC semantic layer, in a semi-automated manner.

Custom Example

For our complex BW pilot case, we are using both a user exit and a custom ABAP authorization variable that is used in one of the custom BW queries that is based on the data loaded from the 2LIS_02_ITM Datasource (Purchase Order Item data).

In SAP BW, a BEx query mainly defines how data is consumed in BW reports. In SAP Datasphere, in Business Data Cloud, this functionality is handled by Analytic Models. The analytic models support filters, input parameters, hierarchies, and measures, just like BW Queries, but with a streamlined experience for both developers and business users.

The Analytic Model in SAP BDC serves as a semantic layer, exposing business logic and relationships in a form that is directly consumable by business users.

The analytic model allows us to create and define multi-dimensional models to provide data for analytical purposes to answer different business questions. The sources for analytic models are facts, which can contain dimensions, texts and hierarchies. Please note that some terms and concepts are used differently in analytic models to align more closely with the terminology in SAP Analytics Cloud stories:

  • "Input parameters” are called “variables” in analytic models.
  • "Attributes” are called “dimensions” in analytic models.

The graphic below shows the role of the analytic model within SAP Datasphere in SAP Business Data Cloud:

 In our example, we are using a custom analytic model on top of the SQL view defined as semantic usage “fact” that we have created in SAP Datasphere within BDC in the previous blog Blog 4 - Beyond Data Warehousing: AI-assisted migration of Business Logic from BW 7.5 to BDC Part 1.

First, let’s look at the custom analytic model creation in SAP Business Data Cloud.

Custom Analytic Model

In the previous blog, we saved the SQL view for the Purchase Order Item as semantic usage “fact”, which is like the Star schema in SAP BW and it includes measures, From the same SQL view defined as semantic usage “fact”, we can create our analytic model automatically, as shown in below picture, that will also inherit all associations defined in the previous SQL view. 

Like in SAP BW star schema, in SAP Datasphere a table or view to any other table or view at any level of the data layer can have multiple associations which mainly define semantic relationships between entities. In our BW pilot case example, we have defined 3 associations in the Purchasing SQL view.

  1. The 1st association is based on BW Vendor master data attributes that is generated via the Data Product Generator. More details can be found here.

  2. The 2nd association is based on standard Time dimension from SAP Datasphere. In SAP Datasphere space, time tables and dimension views can be created automatically to provide standardized time data for our analyses (more details SAP Help Portal).

  3. The 3rd association is based on BW Custom hierarchy uploaded to SAP Datasphere.

So now, when we create our analytic model automatically from the SQL view for the Purchase Order Item, including the associations,  we can view below results, similar to the BW extended star schema. 

The analytic model can include different types of measures (calculated, restricted, standard and exception aggregation, count distinct, currency conversion and non-cumulative), dimensions, structures of members, variables, data access controls, filters, similar to SAP BW BEx query.

Below is an example of

  • Calculated and Restricted measures (like BW Calculated / Restricted Key Figures)
  • Dimensions (like BW Master Data Attributes / Navigation attributes)
  • Structures of members (like BW Key Figure Structures)
  • Variables (like BW Variables)
  • Data Access Controls (like BW Authorization Variables / Analysis Authorizations)

Now let’s deep dive at the 1st variable Date, which is a dynamic default single value filter variable (equivalent to a BW user exit variable).

There are different types of variables to choose from. In our case this is a filter variable that mainly refers to the Date attribute. When a story is opened, the variable dialog shows the filter for the attribute. Now the variable can be filled by different types. In our example, we created a dynamic default variable that mainly allows us to get a derived value in the parameter dialog list when opening the analytic preview, and in the variable prompt for an SAP Analytics Cloud story. For Dynamic Default, the value is derived before the variable prompt pops up, which means the values appear in the variable prompt.

The lookup entity is a table or view that we want to join to our input entity, in order to enrich it. We can reuse the lookup entity in other analytic models as well similarly to SAP BW where the same user exit variable is used across different BEx Queries. Therefore, changes can be governed centrally in the lookup entity if required.

In our example the derived value is mainly the previous year date based on current date, which is mainly the same day as today for last year.

Example of Custom SAP BW User Exit Variable

In our case, the custom ABAP variable (user exit variable) is retrieving the same date as current date but for last year, with below ABAP code.

DATA: 
l_s_range type rrs0_s_range_c, 
l_initdate type sy-datum,
l_prevdate type sy-datum,
l_year type N length 4,
l_monthday type N length 4.

CASE i_vnam.
  WHEN  'ZDATE_LY'. 
        IF i_step = 1. 
  l_initdate = sy-datum.
  l_year = l_initdate+0(4) – 1.
  l_monthday = l_initdate+4(4).
  CONCATENATE l_year l_monthday INTO l_prevdate.
  l_s_range-low  = l_prevdate.
  l_s_range-sign = 'I'.
  l_s_range-opt  = 'EQ'.
      APPEND l_s_range TO e_t_range.
         ENDIF.
ENDCASE.

 AI-assisted replication of BW ABAP code to SQL Code

To transform the code in BDC we need to migrate the ABAP routine. For that we will use SAP AI Launchpad by also allowing human in the loop (HITL) to review and improve the AI-assisted generated code. Again, the main target audience for this role is a Developer with SQL knowledge. The response from SAP AI Launchpad is the below:

Now that we have the code generated, we can use it in the data builder layer in SAP Datasphere in BDC. Please find below the steps:

  1. Copy the generated code from SAP AI Launchpad
  2. Create a SQL script table function in SAP Datasphere and paste the code
  3. Replace the naming as per your variable name
  4. Check validity of the code and make any other modifications required
  5. Save the SQL script table function as Relational Dataset Semantic Usage and expose for consumption.
  6. Test the data preview.

Import of BW Authorizations to SAP Datasphere in SAP Business Data Cloud

Now let’s look at how we can migrate authorizations and roles from SAP BW into SAP Datasphere in Business Data Cloud befrore we share an example of an SAP BW Authorization User Exit Variable.

SAP Datasphere and SAP Analytics Cloud share the same authorization rules regarding the users and roles created. This blog describes the whole security framework in detail. In SAP Business Data Cloud in SAP Datasphere, we can import SAP BW permissions via the Data Builder menu as shown below:

We select the BW Connection (established natively in SAP Datasphere via the DP Agent to SAP BW Private Cloud edition (PCE) or on-premise BW).

SAP BW is mainly populating the user roles and authorizations in RSDWC_RSEC_DAC. This is a specific table in SAP BW systems from version 7.5 SP16+ used to store analysis authorizations for export to SAP Datasphere. The table stores a snapshot of defined analysis authorizations (both the object structure and associated filter values) within BW. It acts as a bridge for replicating authorization information to external analytics tools, enabling consistent data access control across different platforms. Entries in this table are generated using the SAP transaction code RSDWC_DAC_RSEC_GEN within BW.  A wizard in SAP Datasphere facilitates importing the permissions, automatically mapping BW users to Datasphere users (often using email addresses for matching). More details can be found here.

Example of Custom SAP BW Authorization User Exit Variable

Now let’s look at the example of an SAP BW Authorization User Exit Variable, which is equivalent to the Data Access Control (DAC) framework to implement row-level security. 

In our recent BW pilot case, we used the variable Purchasing group, which is a dynamic default single value filter variable (equivalent to an authorization user exit variable in BW):

The logic of the authorization of the user is mainly maintained in a SQL script table function as shown below that mainly utilizes the table that is used also in the Data Access Control.Please note, the below SQL code is an example intended for demonstration. In our pilot case, we used a more complex authorization business logic. 

The Data Access Control Table for purshasing looks like below:

 When we do model preview, we test the results.

In our example below we have included in our analytic model the data access control based on the material group hierarchy.

 

This specific data access control will trigger the authorizations that we are allowed to see as users on this analytic model based on the hierarchy nodes that we have provided for the material group. As we can see from below screen we are only viewing the "other" component of the material group hierarchy and we are not allowed to see the rest view.

 

 

 The analytic model and all components demonstrated above can also be consumed by the SAP Analytics Cloud Stories with live connectivity. Here below is an example of a simple SAP Analytics Cloud Dashboard or Story based on the same analytic model we showcased earlier with the same variables used. 

In SAP Analytics Cloud we can also add additional input parameters in the story level if required.

These data now can also be shared as a custom data product and consumed in the open data Lakehouse architecture environment. This will be demonstrated in the next blog post of the series.

Alternative Solutions

There are a few more options to replicate BW BEx Query logic from BW 7.5 on HANA to SAP Business Data Cloud. One of them is mainly to generate the SAP HANA Views from the BW system (SAP Help ) or leverage the Data Product Generator in BW by using BW query as Infoprovider (Blog-2).

In addition, the SAP BW Migration Assistant in SAP Business Data Cloud will be available Q4.2026 (Roadmap) that translates data flows into corresponding entities in SAP Datasphere. The main benefit is reducing manual effort in recreating data flows for SAP BW from scratch in SAP Datasphere within SAP Business Data Cloud. 

Conclusion

SAP BW customers can now have a clear path to maximize their existing investments while embracing the future of enterprise analytics and AI. Through SAP Business Data Cloud, organizations can preserve their proven SAP BW 7.5 on HANA assets and seamlessly integrate them with modern cloud-native capabilities, creating a scalable, future-ready architecture that delivers immediate value without requiring complete system redesign or rebuild.

Please stay tuned for the 6th blog post in this series, ‘Blog 6 – How to modernize SAP BW 7.5 APD’s in SAP BDC Architecture and create custom Data Products’.

entry.title: SAP Developer News for April 30th, 2026
entry.content:
Description

🚨 NPM supply chain attack campaign, SAP packages affected

  • Step Security post https://www.stepsecurity.io/blog/a-mini-shai-hulud-has-appeared

CAP Apr 2026 release

  • Release notes https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/releases/2026/apr26

The refreshed SAP Architecture Center

  • Announcement: https://architecture.learning.sap.com/news/2026/04/22/introducing-the-refreshed-sap-architecture-center
  • SAP Architecture Center: https://architecture.learning.sap.com/

CAP AI Plugin for Node.js

  • GitHub Repo: https://github.com/cap-js/ai

DYK #14 CW18

  • More Info: https://help.sap.com/docs/build-apps/service-guide/trigger-event

CHAPTER TITLES

0:00 Intro

0:10 🚨 NPM supply chain attack campaign, SAP packages affected

1:20 CAP Apr 2026 release

10:08 The refreshed SAP Architecture Center

11:13 CAP AI Plugin for Node.js

11:57 DYK #14 CW18

Transcription

00;00;00;17 - 00;00;06;15
Announcer Guy
This is the SAP Developer News for April 30th, 2026.

00;00;06;18 - 00;00;09;26
DJ Adams
This is an important announcement relating to a security

00;00;09;27 - 00;00;45;02
DJ Adams
incident concerning some SAP packages on NPM, specifically MBT, the Multi Target Application Build tool and some of the database related modules in the cap.js scope. SQLite, Postgres and DB service. The incident a software supply chain attack in the context of the Shai Hulud campaign, occurred yesterday and has the full attention of teams inside of SAP who were engaged within a couple of hours of it happening.

00;00;45;04 - 00;01;21;06
DJ Adams
For more details on the affected package versions, how it happened, how to find out if you're affected and what you should do right now, you should head over to this Step security post, which has an excellent analysis and detailed action plan. We'll leave you with the details here of the specific package versions that have been compromised, and strongly recommend you take the appropriate actions described in the Posts Recovery Steps section.

00;01;21;08 - 00;01;53;25
Also DJ Adams
The latest CAP Release, April 2026 is out. This brings cap Node.js to 9.9, as well as the CDS-DK to 9.9, CAP Java to 4.9, and the CDS compiler to 6.9. Perhaps most significantly, though, is that this release looks forward to the upcoming next major release that CDS ten, in that some of the features and facilities introduced this time around are to help you prepare and move to CAP 10.

00;01;53;28 - 00;02;18;17
Also DJ Adams
Keeping your cap developments up to date is an important aspect in maintenance, and if you want to learn more, we've got a recent Expert Session episode for you on this very topic that you can bookmark and watch later. Okay, let's start looking at a few highlights of this current release. Starting with these details relating to help you plan for this move to CAP 10 later this year.

00;02;18;20 - 00;02;56;23
Also DJ Adams
First, Node.js support Node.js 22 becomes the minimum supported runtime after all will be at Node.js 26. As the lights version this coming October already, and in one of a number of consistent moves towards fewer external dependencies, the runtime native node SQLite library is now the default driver for SQLite. Next up in the area of help with release transitioning is an overhaul of feature and behavior.

00;02;56;23 - 00;03;36;08
Also DJ Adams
Flex. The CAP team have put in a lot of effort to put structure, transparency, and process around the changes that we need to pay attention to and act upon from release to release. The feature flags, each of which describe a change in behavior, have been organized and follow a lifecycle. When a flag is first introduced, the existing current or soon to be old behavior is the default value in the transition to the next major release, the new behavior becomes the default, and then in the release beyond that, the flag is removed.

00;03;36;10 - 00;04;15;06
Also DJ Adams
This means you can comfortably check in plenty of time and adjust as you need to before it's too late. Related to this is the convenient collection of flags in the try it out section, which you can add to your configuration to test your services and apps in CDS 9.9 but with CDS 10 behavior to see what breaks. Still, on the topic of looking ahead to cap ten, CAP Java will move to major version five supporting Spring Boot four, and there's a migration guide that you can use to prepare for that move.

00;04;15;08 - 00;04;44;10
Also DJ Adams
Last, but certainly not least, in this context of breaking changes, be aware that the CDS compiler now emits a hard error and has done actually since compiler version 6.5, where it encounters a security related annotation of something that doesn't exist. Now, moving beyond the topic of preparation for CDS ten, there still plenty to report in this April 2026 release.

00;04;44;10 - 00;05;11;17
Also DJ Adams
Let me pick out some highlights for you on the topic of vector embeddings. There's enhanced support for the Java on time and also in the CDS compiler, the CDL built in vector type, which in the past was only available for use with Hana, can now be used with H2 and SQLite for local development, and there's also beta support for Postgres.

00;05;11;19 - 00;05;50;02
Also DJ Adams
In addition, the Java runtime now also supports cosine similarity and L2 distance functions for similarity search and retrieval. Augmented generation with vector embeddings. Now, moreover, there's a new beta vector embedding function supported by the Java runtime that can work on text data directly in HANA. Now let's look at some CDS language updates in this release. Declarative constraints introduced at the end of last year are now generally available GA ready for production use.

00;05;50;04 - 00;06;25;20
Also DJ Adams
Note that there's a small change with this GA release regarding error codes. Now the CDS family of languages is becoming more powerful and expressive every month. In particular, when combined view definitions in CDL can now be extended using CQL clauses. Also, while we're familiar with using the Extend directive to add, say, elements and annotations to our definitions, we can now also use it to add enum values to derived enum types.

00;06;25;23 - 00;06;53;26
Also DJ Adams
There are updates in the Node.js runtime to many of them in a particular area, which I'll introduce by means of a quote from a computer scientist, programing language pioneer, and first recipient of the Alan Turing Award. Alan Perlis. Quote A software system can best be designed if the testing is interlaced with the designing, instead of being used after the design.

00;06;53;29 - 00;07;49;29
Also DJ Adams
So this release brings CDS test version 1.0, which will play a major role in the CAP product portfolio and related. In this release, we see an upgrade to version six of the latest Chai assertion library, added support for V test, which you should possibly consider as the primary choice for your testing framework these days, and following the same philosophy as we've heard about already, the dependency on the third party library, Axios is now replaced with Node.js native fetch API in this testing context, and the fetch API also means that we can simplify our development setups by relying on that also for remote communication scenarios instead of the SAP, which of course is still required for

00;07;49;29 - 00;08;18;08
Also DJ Adams
hybrid and production scenarios due to, for example, the important use of BTP destinations. There are also some changes and updates in the Java runtime, most notably regarding how services are served now. An application service will only serve by default. OData v2 and OData v4. There's a new property CDS protocols defaults that you can set for others instead in other dates.

00;08;18;08 - 00;08;49;27
Also DJ Adams
For other updates for the Java runtime, there is now support for CQL date and time functions to extract info from a timestamp and calculate durations, there are improvements on how you can specify and control protocol and path configurations. There's new documentation for adopting brownfield projects in the context of Spring Boot, and there are efficiency improvements in how draft deletion is done.

00;08;49;29 - 00;09;20;05
Also DJ Adams
So last but certainly not least, here are some highlights from updates in this release for the cap plugins area. Version 2.0 of the Change Tracking plugin bring significant performance benefits. Offers a new hierarchical view of changes using a tree table, and as support for CXL in the changelog annotation. There's also a migration plan available for moving to this new version.

00;09;20;08 - 00;10;01;23
Also DJ Adams
There are lots of updates and improvements in the area of attachments in both the Java and Node.js runtime context. And finally, there are two new plugins a print service plugin which, as you can probably guess, integrates with SAP Print Service and a process plugin which offers facilities for interaction with SAP build process automation. There's quite a release. Make sure you head on over to the detailed release page in Capire paying particular attention to the support and facilities that I've mentioned made available for the upcoming major release in June.

00;10;01;26 - 00;10;07;13
Also DJ Adams
Link to that copy page is in the description.

00;10;07;15 - 00;10;31;24
Antonio Maradiaga
SAP developers. The SAP Architecture Center just got a big refresh and it's worth checking it out. The website has been redesigned, search and navigation have been improved, but honestly, the most exciting part is in the new look. But the content. There are new practical architectures covering agents, AI and AI agents on SAP, BTP using Joule Studio, and the SAP cloud SDK for AI.

00;10;31;27 - 00;10;57;17
Antonio Maradiaga
There's also a whole section on integrated and extending duel across your landscape with SAP for HANA and SuccessFactors. Also, remember that the Architecture Center is a community driven, open source project. We can all contribute to it. The team has streamline the process with better docs, clear guidelines, and a VS Code integration to make it easier for you to submit your own reference architectures.

00;10;57;17 - 00;11;07;28
Antonio Maradiaga
So if you build something cool, please share it. Check out piers announcement on the SAP Architecture Center link in the description.

00;11;08;00 - 00;11;13;27
Antonio Maradiaga
Asta luego.

00;11;14;00 - 00;11;15;01
Kevin Riedelsheimer
Hey, everybody.

00;11;15;03 - 00;11;45;18
Kevin Riedelsheimer
Today's news item comes to you straight from the beautiful city of Budapest. The CAP team just released a brand new plugin for cap-js which is the AI plugin, and it allows you to easily to connect to SAP AI Core and handle APIs from SAP AI Core straight from your CAP service. And as well, it gives you out of the support for field recommendations on your UI using SAP Rapid-One model.

00;11;45;19 - 00;11;58;29
Kevin Riedelsheimer
So if you're interested in checking out this brand new plugin, head over to GitHub and read everything about it. Try it out right now. Today.

00;11;59;02 - 00;12;21;21
Shrini Neelamegam
Did you know you can define logic in the global canvas and trigger it from anywhere in your app? Global canvas is ideal for managing app wide events, shape changes, and reusable flows. It has no user interface and the logic defined in the global canvas can be triggered from anywhere in the app, making it a powerful tool for centralizing shared functionality.

00;12;21;24 - 00;12;45;06
Shrini Neelamegam
If your app has repeating logic, for example, enabling an X button after user selection, you can define it once in the global canvas and trigger it across pages to avoid duplication. You can define a receive event in the global canvas to handle the state changes, and then trigger that event from buttons on the individual pages, eliminating the need to duplicate the same event.

00;12;45;06 - 00;12;47;11
Shrini Neelamegam
Handling logic across multiple pages.

entry.title: Building an AI Agent for SAP Maintenance using MCP, CAP and Claude Desktop
entry.content:
From Transactions to Conversations: Connecting Claude Desktop to SAP S/4HANA via MCP

A few weekends ago I sat down with a question I've been carrying for months: how fast is generative AI actually going to change the way we interact with SAP? Not in the marketing sense. In the day-to-day, "open IW38, set the filters, export to Excel" sense.

I didn't want to write another opinion piece. I wanted to build something and see for myself.

This post is the result: a working proof of concept, two short videos of it running, and an honest reflection on what I think it means for everyone in the SAP world, users, developers, consultants, support teams.

 Why I Built This

The trigger was the Model Context Protocol (MCP), the open standard Anthropic released to let AI assistants talk to external systems through a clean, structured interface. The moment I understood how it worked, one thought wouldn't leave me alone: if Claude Desktop can call any tool, why is it still asking people to navigate transactions to get SAP data?

So I picked the SAP module I know best, Plant Maintenance, and built a bridge.

 What the Project Does

The proof of concept lets a user ask Claude Desktop questions like these, in natural language, and get answers pulled live from SAP S/4HANA:

  • "I have a meeting in 5 minutes about EDDY Pumps. Give me a quick summary of all open orders."
  • "Is there any order related to an oil leak? What's planned to fix it?"
  • "Equipment 10001949, does it have more than one open order? Show me all of them."
  • "Which orders have priority 1 in plant 1010?"

No SAP GUI. No Fiori. No transaction codes. No filter screens. The user just asks.

See It Running

I recorded two short videos showing the assistant pulling real data from S/4HANA:

Video 1 - Summary before a meeting:


Video 2 - Investigating a leak:

 The Architecture (Briefly)

 

For those who want the technical picture:

  • SAP CAP on Node.js exposes an MCP-compliant HTTP endpoint on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry
    A small local script (mcp-stdio.js) acts as a stdio bridge between Claude Desktop and the BTP server
    The server queries SAP S/4HANA via OData (using SAP Business Accelerator Hub for the sandbox)
    Claude Desktop sees it as a standard MCP server with tools it can call

  •  

get_maintenance_orders
get_maintenance_order_detail
get_maintenance_order_operations

At runtime, Claude Desktop sees a small set of domain-specific tools exposed via MCP:

The full code, deploy steps, and configuration example are in the repo. Lessons learned (CAP routing quirks, Express middleware gotchas, why you need the stdio proxy at all) are documented there too.

  • Step 2 - configure Claude Desktop

    Open %APPDATA%\Claude\claude_desktop_config.json and add:

 

{
  "mcpServers": {
    "sap-maintenance": {
      "command": "node",
      "args": [
        "C:\\path\\to\\mcp-maintenance-cap\\mcp-stdio.js"
      ]
    }
  }
}

 Now the Honest Part What This Could Mean for the SAP World

This is where I want to slow down. Because the demo is fun, but the implications are bigger.

For End Users
The maintenance manager who today depends on someone, or on remembering filter combinations she learned five years ago, to pull a report? She just asks. The supervisor who wanted to know which equipment has the most open notifications but never had time to learn the t-code? He just asks.

This is, unambiguously, a win for the user. The system finally bends to the human, instead of the other way around.

 For Developers and Consultants

Here's where it gets interesting, and where I find myself thinking out loud.

If a well-designed AI agent can answer most operational questions through natural language, what happens to the parts of our work that revolve around teaching users to navigate the system, building the 47th custom report, or writing the "how to filter IW38" page of a training manual?

I don't think those things disappear overnight. Enterprise software doesn't move at consumer-AI speed. But I do think the shape of the work changes, and it changes faster than most of us are planning for.

 For Configuration, Training, Projects, and Support

A few honest questions I'm sitting with:

  • If an AI agent can explain why an order is in a given status by reading the master data on the fly, how much classic L1/L2 support is still needed?
  • If users no longer need to learn screens, how much of a typical training plan survives?
  • If a consultant's value moves from "I know where the field is" to "I know how to design the agent and the data behind it," what does the next generation of SAP consultant look like?
  • When does this happen, 2 years, 5 years, 10 years? I genuinely don't know. Anyone who tells you they do is selling something.

 What's Next

The same pattern works for any SAP module. The maintenance use case was where I started because it's mine, but you can imagine equivalents for MM (stock and POs), SD (orders and deliveries), QM (inspections), or FI/CO (cost centers and postings). The architecture doesn't care which OData service is on the other end.

I'd love to see this turn into a community conversation. What modules would you try first? What does the "agent-first SAP" workflow look like in your area? Where do you see the real risks?

Try It, Break It, Tell Me About It
The full project code, deploy steps, lessons learned, and videos are open on GitHub:

https://github.com/bernardcaldas/mcp-maintenance-cap 

Suggestions, pull requests, and issues are very welcome on the repo. Critiques, corrections, and "you missed this" are equally welcome.

We're early. None of us has the full picture. But I'd rather build small things and learn in public than wait for someone else to define what AI + SAP looks like.

Thanks for reading.

entry.title: CAP Developers - Call to Action to mitigate and Apply Solution provided in SAP Note 3747787
entry.content:
On April 29, 2026, four malicious open-source package versions were distributed into the NPM ecosystem. These malicious versions appear to exfiltrate information, such as credentials, and attempt to propagate into downstream software packages as well as adjacent software repositories when installed on a system.

If you are uncertain whether your systems have been affected, it is crucial to act promptly. Begin by following the mitigation steps outlined to maintain your environment’s security. Promptly taking these actions will help protect your systems and data from potential risks.

Other Terms

  • MBT
  • NPM
  • CAP
  • SAP Cloud Application Programming Model
  • MTA Build Tool

List of compromised NPM package versions:

  • · @cap-js/sqlite - v2.2.2
  • · @cap-js/postgres - v2.2.2
  • · @cap-js/db-service - v2.10.1
  • · mbt@1.2.48

Solution

If you have identified that you may be affected, perform the following measures via provided SAP Note below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003747787

SAP Support Ticket Component : BC-XS-CDX-NJS

Potential references:

  • https://www.aikido.dev/blog/mini-shai-hulud-has-appeared
  • https://www.stepsecurity.io/blog/a-mini-shai-hulud-has-appeared
  • https://www.mend.io/blog/shai-hulud-sap-cap-supply-chain-attack-claude-code/
  • https://onapsis.com/blog/sap-cap-mini-shai-hulud-supply-chain-attack/
  • https://snyk.io/de/blog/bun-based-stealer-hits-sap-cap-js-mbt-npm-packages/

entry.title: SAP Business AI in Consumer Products - Partner live expert session
entry.content:
AI in Consumer Products is moving fast.  And many partners are asking the same question: where do I start with customers?

This live expert session is built to give you clear answers.  You will get a grounded view of the Consumer Products sector.  Key trends.  Real pressures your customers face.  Then we go deeper into SAP Business AI with practical use cases and customer references you can reuse in your conversations.

Plus, we walk through how to position AI.  Who to engage on the client side.  What resonates in early discussions.  And yes, there is a live demo on SDE 2.0 so you can see it in action, not just slides.

The thing is, AI sales cycles reward clarity and confidence.  This session helps you build both.

Join us and leave with ideas you can apply right away.

Register now.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/apr/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00013973.html

entry.title: How SAP Business Network Is Taking Shape on SAP BTP
entry.content:
In this blog post you will learn how SAP Business Network is being reshaped on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) and what that means for teams working across procurement and supply chain networks.

It highlights why SAP is making this shift, what’s changing behind the scenes with data, integration, and intelligence, and how these updates can simplify collaboration while supporting future growth. 

If you want to understand the direction SAP Business Network is taking, what capabilities are being unlocked on BTP, and how these changes could impact your current landscape and future planning, this guide on Supply Chain Management Value Map is worth exploring in full.

Enhance Your SAP Experience with Value Maps

Rachana is a Topic Expert for SAP Enterprise Support value maps, specializing in Spend Management. With over 15 years at SAP, she brings deep experience from a range of customer-facing roles.
