entry.title: SAP Joule Studio MCP Setup Guide
entry.content:
Building a Custom MCP Server for Joule Studio on SAP BTP: What Worked, What Broke, and What I Learned

MCP support in Joule Studio opens up a strong integration path on SAP BTP. On paper, the setup looks simple: expose tools through an MCP server, connect that server in Joule Studio, and let the agent invoke backend capabilities through a standard interface. In practice, getting from “the server is up” to “the agent is actually using the tools” can take more than just wiring an endpoint.

In my case, the goal was to expose structured enterprise data and document references from multiple backend services through a custom MCP server. I wanted Joule to work against live backend systems instead of relying only on prompt reasoning. That meant building a Node.js-based MCP server on SAP BTP, deploying it on Cloud Foundry, connecting it to destinations, and validating that Joule Studio could discover and invoke the tools.

What started as a straightforward implementation turned into a deeper exercise in destination handling, authentication patterns, MCP lifecycle behavior, and compatibility troubleshooting. This post captures that journey: what I tried first, why I landed on Node.js, what issues showed up along the way, and what I learned from the full implementation path.

Why I Did Not Start with a Full MCP Server

My first instinct was to use a faster scaffolded route and go straight into MCP. But the requirement here was not just to expose tools. The implementation had to fit well with SAP BTP destinations, Cloud Foundry bindings, XSUAA-backed flows, and backend service calls that needed to stay aligned with the SAP destination model.

That is where Node.js became the more practical choice. Using SAP Cloud SDK for JavaScript made destination handling much easier to control, especially once authentication started evolving. It also gave me a cleaner way to iterate on runtime behavior in Cloud Foundry.

Even then, I did not go directly into the final MCP flow. I first built a lightweight wrapper with temporary validation routes so I could prove that the backend calls themselves were working before layering MCP protocol handling on top.

Why the Wrapper First Used Temporary /test Routes

This ended up being one of the most useful decisions in the implementation.

Before turning the wrapper into a real MCP server, I exposed temporary routes to validate the backend calls individually. That let me test the destination execution separately from MCP transport behavior. In other words, I could answer a basic question first: do the backend service calls work at all?

That helped a lot, because several issues that initially looked like MCP problems were actually destination or authentication problems. By validating backend calls independently, I could isolate transport-level issues from service-call issues much faster.

Once all four calls were working, I removed those routes and replaced the wrapper façade with the actual MCP endpoint on /mcp.

Deploying the Wrapper on Cloud Foundry

The runtime model was Cloud Foundry, so the wrapper had to remain simple and lightweight. The deployment itself was straightforward, but what mattered more was how often I had to fall back to Cloud Foundry inspection commands to remove guesswork.

The two commands that helped most throughout the build were:

cf services
cf env <app-name>

cf services helped identify the actual service instance names in the space.

cf env helped confirm which service instances were bound to the target CAP application and what the runtime was actually seeing.

Those two commands were much more valuable than trying to infer bindings manually. Once I started using them consistently, it became easier to stabilize the destination and authentication configuration.

The main deployment loop stayed simple:

cf push
cf logs <app-name> --recent

The Destination Model That Finally Worked

One of the key implementation choices was to keep the MCP server thin and let destinations handle backend access. In the final model, the wrapper did not rely on hardcoded full service URLs. Instead, it called named destinations and used relative resource paths for the actual backend operations.

There were two backend destinations involved:

  • one for folder or DMS-related content
  • one for processor-style service calls such as failure codes, equipment context, and repair history

Separately, there was also a dedicated Joule-facing MCP destination. This was important: the Joule-facing destination should point to the MCP server itself, not directly to CAP.

One practical rule turned out to matter more than expected:

Do not append /mcp in the destination URL. Keep /mcp only in the Joule Studio advanced path setting.

That small separation helped avoid confusion during troubleshooting and made it easier to verify whether the request was reaching the raw MCP app correctly.

Where the Real Complexity Started: Authentication

Authentication took the longest to stabilize.

At first, some of the flows still assumed a browser-like entry model. That became problematic once the entry point shifted to a technical MCP server. A pattern that works in a browser-backed setup does not always translate cleanly when Joule is invoking a technical destination and expecting the MCP server to orchestrate the rest.

This led to several rounds of troubleshooting:

  • stale destination credentials
  • confusion between browser-authenticated paths and technical backend calls
  • incoming token assumptions that no longer held in the MCP path
  • additional testing around approuter behavior versus the raw Node.js app

The turning point was simplifying the model:

  • the Joule-facing MCP destination was moved to the raw Node.js app
  • the MCP server no longer depended on an incoming bearer token
  • backend destinations handled technical OAuth themselves
  • the MCP server became a thin orchestration layer instead of an authentication-heavy gateway

Once the model became technically consistent end to end, the setup became much easier to reason about.

Converting the Wrapper into a Real MCP Server

After the backend wrapper was stable, I converted it into a proper MCP server on /mcp. The key lifecycle methods implemented were:
  • initialize
  • notifications/initialized
  • tools/list
  • tools/call
One important gap that surfaced during testing was notifications/initialized. Joule could reach the endpoint, but the server still did not progress properly until that notification was explicitly handled. Once support for that lifecycle step was added, tool discovery started working. That was one of the moments where the implementation moved from “the server is technically reachable” to “the server is behaving in a way Joule actually expects.” As part of troubleshooting, I also validated the project dependency state and MCP protocol version alignment. That was important because once Joule started reaching the endpoint, I needed to rule out whether the issue was coming from package or runtime mismatch, or from protocol negotiation, rather than from the backend calls themselves. In the end, those checks helped confirm that discovery was working correctly up to tools/list, and that the remaining blocker was elsewhere.

The Zod Question: Useful to Test, but Not the Root Cause

At one point, a separate question came up around schema style. Since Joule Studio guidance increasingly references the MCP TypeScript/Node SDK package together with Zod-based schemas, I wanted to rule out whether the earlier plain-schema approach was the reason tool execution was not progressing.

So I created a second implementation from scratch:

  • separate app
  • separate destination
  • separate validation flow
  • Zod-based route

That comparison turned out to be very useful.

The result was that both implementations behaved the same way from Joule’s perspective. In both cases, Joule successfully completed:

  • initialize
  • notifications/initialized
  • tools/list

but still did not progress to tools/call.

That was an important finding because it ruled out one major suspicion. In this case, the issue was not simply plain schema versus Zod schema. At least in this case, both implementations were healthy enough for discovery, which pointed the remaining blocker more toward the Joule-side invocation path than toward the MCP server registration model itself.

What I Learned

There are a few things I would carry forward immediately into a similar implementation.

First, I would always validate backend calls independently before turning the server into a full MCP endpoint. The temporary wrapper route phase saved a lot of time.

Second, I would simplify authentication assumptions much earlier. Anything that still implicitly depends on browser-session behavior becomes harder to reason about once an MCP server becomes the actual entry point.

Third, I would treat cf services, cf env, and logs as core implementation tools, not just troubleshooting tools. In Cloud Foundry-based builds, they are what remove ambiguity.

Finally, I would not assume that tool discovery works means tool invocation is solved. Discovery and execution are separate milestones, and this implementation path made that distinction very clear.

A Few Commands That Helped Repeatedly

Project initialization:

mkdir <CLNT>-equipment-mcp-node
cd <CLNT>-equipment-mcp-node
npm init -y
npm install @modelcontextprotocol/sdk @sap-cloud-sdk/http-client @sap/xsenv express @sap/approuter

Cloud Foundry setup and checks:

cf api https://api.cf.eu10.hana.ondemand.com
cf login
cf services
cf env <CLNT>_Equipment_MVP-srv
cf push
cf logs <CLNT>-equipment-mcp-node --recent

Closing Thoughts

Even though this implementation took more troubleshooting than expected, it was still valuable end to end. It helped establish a practical MCP server pattern on SAP BTP, clarified how destinations and authentication should be aligned for a technical entry point, and exposed where the boundary lies between server health and actual tool invocation.

My biggest takeaway is to validate each layer separately: backend calls first, then MCP lifecycle handling, then tool discovery, and only after that, tool invocation. That separation made the implementation far easier to reason about.

And if you find yourself stuck after tools/list, it is worth remembering that by that point, the issue may no longer be your MCP server at all. 

In my case, the MCP server side was validated through discovery, while the remaining gap was on the tool invocation side.

#SAPBTP #JouleStudio #MCP #NodeJS #TypeScript #CloudFoundry #SAPBuild

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere News in April
entry.content:
SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere News in April

SAPPHIRE season is upon us 🌴 and the April releases didn't hold back either — plenty of useful features landing across monitoring, task chains, data products, and more.

Learn more in my top features video in April 2026 on YouTube 📺.

In addition, explore the latest updates in our community news blogs and more.
 

 

My top features in April

Update Action for Installed Data Products

Data products installed in an SAP Datasphere space now have an Update action button, available when new minor versions are released. This improvement streamlines the workflow, providing a more efficient and intuitive process for users tasked with keeping data products current. 
➡️  More information Installing Data Products and Data Product Details.

 

BDC Cockpit Data Product Monitoring

As promised, the BDC Cockpit is being continuously improved. Administrators now get significantly better visibility into SAP-managed data product pipelines — this is the first step of several improvements coming over the next months.

The cockpit now shows the full pipeline status at every step, making it easy to identify exactly where issues occur and act before they impact your business.

 

Local Table Partitioning

In the table editor, you can create partitions on existing SAP-Managed local tables stored in a standard space (with SAP HANA Database (Disk and In-Memory) storage) that already contain data. So previously unpartitioned tables can now be partitioned even if they already carry data. This is particularly useful for large-volume tables where partitioning was not possible at initial creation. 
➡️  More information Partitioning Local Tables and Partitioning Local Tables for Intelligent Applications.

 

Spark Configuration

The Limits to the amount of compute resources that Apache Spark applications can consume when running tasks are set with configurations. In addition to using the provided configurations, which are applied by default to new file spaces, you can now change the default configuration for a task activity in each file space, and create your own configurations.

Be aware, that newly provided default configurations for task activities may allocate fewer resources than those used in previous versions (see SAP Note 3726059), tasks might run more slowly even though they consume less compute. If these new default values don't meet your needs, you can change the default configuration for a task activity in each file space. 
➡️  More information.

 

Cloud Connector Support for Google BigQuery

You can now configure private connectivity for Google BigQuery connections through the Cloud Connector. The Cloud Connector routes data traffic through secure tunnels rather than the public internet, allowing you to use replication flows to write data to Google BigQuery target systems that don't have public endpoints. 
➡️  More information.

 

Task Chains Deletion Types

Task chains now support all deletion types for both local tables and Local Tables (file).

The newly supported deletion activities are:

  • Delete All Records
  • Delete Filtered Records

➡️  More information.

 

Semantic Onboarding of Calc Views from HANA Cloud

You can import calculation views from SAP HANA Cloud as remote tables while preserving semantic information. This helps you leverage existing HANA Cloud models without recreating them, keeping their business context and metadata for consistent data modeling across platforms.

Be aware that as a prerequisite, only calculation views deployed in SAP HANA Cloud version QRC 1/2026 or higher can be imported in SAP Datasphere database version 2025.50 or higher.

To ensure that your calculation view can be imported, deploy the calculation view in the source system with SAP Business Data Cloud Integration Mode set to Flat View or Construction Kit.

➡️  More information

  • Importing Entities with Semantics from SAP HANA Cloud
  • SAP HANA Connections
 

Analytic Model — Inherited and Local Properties

A small but useful addition to the analytic model. For analytic models that use another analytic model as source, you can switch on Show Inherited Elements in the properties panel, to display the inherited elements. That makes it easy to distinguish between local and inherited properties. 
➡️  More information.

 

Improved Monitoring Tool Navigation

The Monitoring tool now features an enhanced navigation menu for easier access to key monitoring pages:

  • System and Spaces – Monitor system performance, storage, tasks, and out-of-memory issues across all spaces. Track storage consumption and Apache Spark usage.
  • Capacities Monitoring – Track monthly and daily capacity unit consumption against your subscription and download detailed hourly data.
  • Task Logs – Review task execution history and performance.
  • Expensive Statement Logs – Monitor SQL statements exceeding configured thresholds.
  • Elastic Compute Nodes - Monitor elastic compute nodes key performance figures.
  • Data Integration – Monitor, schedule, and run data replication tasks for remote tables and views. Manage flows and task chains.

That consolidates all the monitoring activities into one menu section. 
➡️  More information.

 

 

SAPPHIRE 2026 — Let's Meet in Orlando

SAP SAPPHIRE 2026 is just around the corner! I'll be in Orlando presenting the introductory session on SAP Business Data Cloud — a 20-minute guided tour of what it is, how it's positioned, and where it fits into SAP's data and analytics strategy. I'm running it twice:

📅 Monday, May 11 | 1:30 PM – 1:50 PM EDT

📅 Tuesday, May 12 | 4:00 PM – 4:20 PM EDT

With 30+ sessions on Business Data Cloud at this year's SAPPHIRE, there's a lot to explore. You'll also find us at the SAP Business Data Cloud booth with six demo stations and hands-on sessions. No excuse not to stop by if you're in Orlando.

Can't make it in person? All content will be available online — and of course, I'll keep publishing the monthly newsletter right here. 
🔗  Session link  |  🔗  My SAPPHIRE blog

 

Webinar Series: The Next Era of Business Data

The last session of the great series has just been streamed on YouTube. In case you could not make it live, all sessions are recorded and I added the links to the recordings below.

🔹Jan 29 | Discover SAP Business Data Cloud in 2026 - recording

🔹Feb 05 | The Smart Path to SAP BW Modernization - recording

🔹Feb 19 | Empowering SAP Datasphere Users with SAP Business Data Cloud Innovations - recording

🔹Feb 26 | From Data to Decisions: Analytics with SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP BDC - recording

🔹Mar 12 | From Data Silos to Business Insight: The Power of BDC Connect - recording

🔹Mar 19 | An Architect's Blueprint for SAP Business Data Cloud as the Foundation for Enterprise AI - recording

🔹Mar 26 | Design. Govern. Deliver. Data Products - Data Product Studio - recording

👉  NEW 🔹 Apr 16 | Breaking Down the SAP BDC Pricing Model - recording

 

More blogs about SAP BDC and Datasphere to check out 👇

  • Blog 5 - Beyond Data Warehousing: AI-assisted migration of Business Logic from BW 7.5 to BDC Part 2
  • Meet Me at SAPPHIRE — Let's Talk SAP Business Data Cloud 🚀
  • Best Practices Recommendations for SAP Datasphere
  • SAP Analytics Cloud in SAP Business Data Cloud: Your Guide to the Latest Features & Enhancements
  • SAP Sapphire 2026, top agenda picks for analytics practitioners
  • SAP Saphire Virtual - Big ideas. Bold innovation. Real impact.
  • SAP Business Data Cloud: Best practices for SAP Business Warehouse modernization miniseries
  • Managing Parent-Child Hierarchies in SAC: A Datasphere-Driven Approach
  • SoCal Community Event: SAP Business Data Cloud "From Pipelines to Data Products"
  • SAC AutoSum Custom Widget — Excel's Status Bar, Native in SAC with Full Code Listing
  • Provisioning of Business Data Cloud: SAP HANA Cloud
  • Beyond Joule Capabilities: A Knowledge Graph Driven Runtime for SAP Business AI
  • Building a Real AI Agent: Natural Language Modeling for SAP Datasphere with Vibe Coding
  • Key figure / column based security in Datasphere
  • The Controller's Favourite: A Business Calculator Custom Widget for SAP Analytics Cloud Tables
  • SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) Onboarding
  • Update on SAP HANA Cloud positioning and commercialization in context of SAP Business Data Cloud
  • Harnessing SAP Business Data Cloud for the administrator persona
  • Sizing the SAP Datasphere Object Store
  • Inside SAP IT 2026: Real Stories, Real Lessons, Real SAP – Live on YouTube
  • From Dashboards to Decisions: Extending SAP Analytics Cloud with Custom Widgets
  • How to Configure and Install People Intelligence in SAP Business Data Cloud
  • SAP Revenue Growth Management Integrated with SAP BDC: Seamless Data Integration & Governed Insights
  • SAP Data & AI Newsletter
  • From Raw Data to Data Products: End-to-End Implementation using SAP Business Data Cloud
  • 🚀AI & Claude Code Experiment (Part 2): Building an SAP Analytics Cloud Custom Widget
  • Welcome to Season 10 of SAP Let's Talk Data!
  • Season 10 of Let's Talk Data Podcast Series: A focus on the data professionals that make it happen
  • Hybrid Data Modeling in SAP BDC: a simple Framework for product-oriented architecture
  • Context-Driven Dashboard Experiences: Role-Based Layouts in SAP Analytics Cloud
  • The Handshake at the Heart of the Data Product Economy: Data Contracts with SAP Business Data Cloud
  • SAP Analytics Cloud in Business Data Cloud Achieves IBCS Re-Certification until February 2028!
  • Live webinar – How Brown-Forman modernizes its SAP BW for the AI era
  • 🚀AI & Claude Code Experiment (Part 1): Building an SAP Analytics Cloud Widget Add-On
  • Sharing SAP S/4HANA Data with Databricks Using BDC Connect and Delta Sharing
  • Blog 4 - Beyond Data Warehousing: AI-assisted migration of Business Logic from BW 7.5 to BDC Part 1
  • Why master data management is the foundation of AI and digital transformation
  • Need to Know - Unlocking the Power of AI in SAP Analytics Cloud
  • SAP BW Bridge or Data Product Generator (DPG): What would you choose
  • SAP Datasphere: Extract, Transform & Load (ETL) - Best Practices
  • Creating and Monitoring a New Replication Flow in SAP Datasphere
  • Natural Language Automation for SAP Datasphere with GitHub Copilot
 

Find more information and related blog posts on the topic pages for SAP Business Data Cloud and SAP Datasphere. Also check out the SAP Help for SAP Business Data Cloud and SAP Datasphere, including for troubleshooting and analysis guides, onboarding guides, and more.

entry.title: CPI iFlow Resource Scanner: How I Built a Simple Web Tool Inside CPI to Find Unused Files
entry.content:
As we work on SAP CPI iFlows over time, we collect extra files. A Groovy script gets added for a new feature, then the feature gets descoped. A Message Mapping gets replaced but the old file stays behind. Soon the resources folder is full of XSD schemas and scripts just sitting there doing nothing.

The problem is that CPI does not show us which files are actually being used. The only option is to click through every step on the canvas to check. This takes too much time, and it needs repeating after every change. Worse, checking the canvas manually misses hidden files - XSD schemas and Groovy UDFs used inside Message Mappings are completely invisible from the canvas view.

This post walks through how I build a resource scanner utility that solves this problem. The tool uses Integration Suite APIs to check the canvas, look inside Message Mappings for hidden dependencies, and report exactly which files are unused. It runs entirely inside CPI and shows results in a browser. The post includes all Groovy scripts, architecture diagrams, and lessons learned from edge cases encountered during development.

1.  Architecture - How It Is Built

The tool is split into two iFlows:

  • UI iFlow: Provides the web page. A GET request returns the HTML interface. A POST request (when you click Scan) forwards to the analysis engine via ProcessDirect.

  • Analysis Engine: Does the actual work of checking files. It only accepts ProcessDirect connections, which means it can be reused by other tools later. This separation has already proven useful.

UI iFlow: GET and POST Request Handling

Analysis Engine: Main Integration Process

2.  The Four Steps of Analysis

Analysis Engine: Local Process Detail

2a.  Step 1: Get the Full List of Files

An OData API call retrieves every file in the iFlow's resource folder:

Endpoint:  https://{baseUrl}/api/v1/IntegrationDesigntimeArtifacts
           (Id='{iFlowID}',Version='active')/Resources

The response gets transformed via XSLT to flatten the Atom XML, then a Groovy script sorts files into four lists:

def xml = new XmlSlurper().parseText(message.getBody(String.class))

def groovyCatalog = [], mmapCatalog = [], xsltCatalog = [], xsdCatalog = []

xml.ResourceList.each { item ->
    def name = item.ResourceName?.text()?.trim()
    def type = item.ResourceType?.text()?.trim()
    
    switch (type) {
        case 'groovy': groovyCatalog << name; break
        case 'mmap':   mmapCatalog << name; break
        case 'xslt':   xsltCatalog << name; break
        case 'xsd':    xsdCatalog << name; break
    }
}

message.setProperty("catalog_groovy", groovyCatalog.join(","))
message.setProperty("catalog_mmap",   mmapCatalog.join(","))
message.setProperty("catalog_xslt",   xsltCatalog.join(","))
message.setProperty("catalog_xsd",    xsdCatalog.join(","))

The lists are stored as exchange properties because each subsequent API call overwrites the message body.

2b.  Step 2: Check the Main Canvas

The tool downloads the iFlow ZIP and extracts the .iflw file - the XML representation of the canvas. It searches for files directly attached to steps.

One thing worth noting: the .iflw uses namespaces that vary between CPI versions. Hardcoding prefixes breaks when moving between environments. Using wildcard traversal avoids this:

def xml = new XmlSlurper().parseText(message.getBody(String.class))

def refGroovy = [] as Set, refMmap = [] as Set
def refXslt = [] as Set, refXsd = [] as Set

xml.'**'.findAll { it.name() == 'property' }.each { prop ->
    def key = prop.@key?.toString()
    def val = prop.@value?.toString()

    if (key == 'script' && val.endsWith('.groovy')) 
        refGroovy << val
    
    else if (key == 'mappinguri') {
        def file = val.tokenize('/').last()
        if (file.endsWith('.mmap')) refMmap << file
        else if (file.endsWith('.xsl') || file.endsWith('.xslt')) refXslt << file
    }
    
    else if (key == 'xsd') {
        def file = val.tokenize('/').last()
        if (file.endsWith('.xsd')) refXsd << file
    }
}

message.setProperty("direct_groovy", refGroovy.join(","))
message.setProperty("direct_mmap",   refMmap.join(","))
message.setProperty("direct_xslt",   refXslt.join(","))
message.setProperty("direct_xsd",    refXsd.join(","))

This finds Groovy scripts on Script steps (key='script'), mappings on Mapping steps (key='mappinguri'), and XSDs on XML Validator steps (key='xsd').

2c.  Step 3: Look Inside Message Mappings

This is the step that provides value beyond what manual inspection can do.

Message Mappings hide files inside them. They reference XSD schemas as source and target structures, and Groovy scripts as UDF libraries. None of this appears on the canvas. A schema can look completely unused while being loaded every time a mapping runs.

The tool opens each .mmap file and extracts these hidden references:

  • lnkRole[@role='SOURCE_IFR_MESS'] → source XSD
  • lnkRole[@role='TARGET_IFR_MESS'] → target XSD
  • lnkRole[@role='UsedFuncLib'] → Groovy UDF

A Splitter processes each mapping file. After the Gather step, a script merges the results:

def mappingList = message.getProperty("direct_mmap")?.split(",")?.findAll { it } ?: []
def indirectXsd = [] as Set, indirectGroovy = [] as Set
def deps = [:]

mappingList.each { name ->
    def xsd = message.getProperty("xsd_refs_for_${name}")?.split(",")?.findAll { it } ?: []
    def gvy = message.getProperty("groovy_refs_for_${name}")?.split(",")?.findAll { it } ?: []
    
    indirectXsd.addAll(xsd)
    indirectGroovy.addAll(gvy)
    deps[name] = [xsd: xsd, groovy: gvy]
}

message.setProperty("indirect_xsd", indirectXsd.join(","))
message.setProperty("indirect_groovy", indirectGroovy.join(","))
message.setProperty("mmap_dependencies", new JsonBuilder(deps).toString())


2d.  Step 4: Do the Math

Resource Comparator: Set Arithmetic and Final JSON Assembly

The tool compares the lists. It takes the full catalog from Step 1 and subtracts everything found in Steps 2 and 3:

Unused Groovy = All_Groovy − (Direct_Groovy ∪ Indirect_Groovy_from_Mappings)
Unused MMAP   = All_MMAP − Direct_MMAP
Unused XSLT   = All_XSLT − Direct_XSLT
Unused XSD    = All_XSD − (Direct_XSD_from_Validator ∪ Indirect_XSD_from_Mappings)
def toSet = { prop -> 
    message.getProperty(prop)?.split(",")?.findAll { it }?.toSet() ?: [] as Set 
}

def catG = toSet("catalog_groovy"), catM = toSet("catalog_mmap")
def catX = toSet("catalog_xslt"),   catS = toSet("catalog_xsd")

def dirG = toSet("direct_groovy"),  dirM = toSet("direct_mmap")
def dirX = toSet("direct_xslt"),    dirS = toSet("direct_xsd")
def indS = toSet("indirect_xsd"),   indG = toSet("indirect_groovy")

def usedG = dirG + indG
def usedS = dirS + indS

def output = [
    summary: [
        total:  [groovy: catG.size(), mmap: catM.size(), xslt: catX.size(), xsd: catS.size()],
        used:   [groovy: usedG.size(), mmap: dirM.size(), xslt: dirX.size(), xsd: usedS.size()],
        unused: [groovy: (catG - usedG).size(), mmap: (catM - dirM).size(), 
                 xslt: (catX - dirX).size(), xsd: (catS - usedS).size()]
    ],
    used: [
        direct: [groovy: dirG as List, mmap: dirM as List, xslt: dirX as List, xsd: dirS as List],
        mmap: new JsonSlurper().parseText(message.getProperty("mmap_dependencies") ?: "{}")
    ],
    unused: [groovy: (catG - usedG) as List, mmap: (catM - dirM) as List,
             xslt: (catX - dirX) as List, xsd: (catS - usedS) as List]
]

message.setBody(new JsonBuilder(output).toPrettyString())
message.setHeader("Content-Type", "application/json")

Whatever is left over is an unused file.

3.  Serving HTML from Groovy

If you are building CPI tools, this technique is worth knowing. You do not need an external server to host a web interface.

CPI's HTTPS adapter returns whatever you put in the message body. Set Content-Type: text/html, return an HTML string from Groovy, and the browser renders it as a normal web page:

Message processData(Message message) {
    def httpMethod = message.getHeaders().get("CamelHttpMethod")

    if (httpMethod == "GET") {
        message.setBody(buildPage())
        message.setHeader("Content-Type", "text/html; charset=UTF-8")
        return message
    }
    return message
}

The HTML page includes JavaScript that posts back to the same URL, receives the JSON from the analysis engine, and renders the dashboard. I have reused this pattern for other utilities.

Here is a sample groovy script to serve the WebUI

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message

def Message processData(Message message) {
    
    def httpMethod = message.getHeaders().get("CamelHttpMethod")
    
    if (httpMethod == "GET") {
        message.setBody(buildPage())
        message.setHeader("Content-Type", "text/html; charset=UTF-8")
    }
    // POST path: JSON flows through from ProcessDirect unchanged
    
    return message
}

def String buildPage() {
    return '''<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
<meta charset="UTF-8">
<title>CPI iFlow Resource Scanner</title>
<style>
:root {
  --navy: #0a1628;
  --blue: #0070f2;
  --teal: #0cc4ac;
  --amber: #f59e0b;
  --gray-100: #f2f4f8;
  --gray-600: #68778f;
  --white: #ffffff;
}
* { box-sizing: border-box; margin: 0; padding: 0; }
body { font-family: system-ui, sans-serif; background: var(--gray-100); }
header { background: linear-gradient(135deg, var(--navy), #1a365d); color: var(--white);
         padding: 16px 24px; display: flex; justify-content: space-between; }
.logo { font-weight: 700; font-size: 16px; }
main { max-width: 1200px; margin: 0 auto; padding: 24px; }
.input-bar { background: var(--white); border-radius: 10px; padding: 16px;
             display: flex; gap: 16px; align-items: flex-end; margin-bottom: 24px; }
.field { display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 6px; flex: 1; }
.field label { font-size: 11px; font-weight: 600; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--gray-600); }
.field input, .field select { padding: 10px; border: 1px solid #ddd; border-radius: 6px; }
.btn { background: var(--blue); color: var(--white); border: none; padding: 10px 24px;
       border-radius: 6px; font-weight: 600; cursor: pointer; }
.btn:disabled { opacity: 0.6; }
.summary-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(4, 1fr); gap: 16px; margin-bottom: 24px; }
.metric-card { background: var(--white); border-radius: 10px; padding: 16px;
               border-top: 3px solid var(--blue); }
.metric-card.unused { border-top-color: var(--amber); }
.metric-title { font-size: 12px; color: var(--gray-600); margin-bottom: 12px; }
.metric-value { font-size: 28px; font-weight: 700; }
.metric-label { font-size: 11px; color: var(--gray-600); }
.panels { display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr); gap: 16px; }
.panel { background: var(--white); border-radius: 10px; overflow: hidden; }
.panel-header { padding: 12px 16px; font-weight: 600; border-bottom: 1px solid #eee; }
.panel-body { padding: 12px; }
.resource-item { padding: 6px 8px; font-family: monospace; font-size: 12px; color: #555; }
.resource-item.unused { color: var(--amber); }
#resultsContainer { display: none; }
#resultsContainer.active { display: block; }
</style>
</head>
<body>
<header>
  <div class="logo">CPI iFlow Resource Scanner</div>
  <div style="font-size:12px;opacity:0.7">v1.0</div>
</header>
<main>
  <div class="input-bar">
    <div class="field">
      <label>Tenant</label>
      <select id="tenantSelect"><option>PROD-US</option><option>DEV-US</option></select>
    </div>
    <div class="field" style="flex:2">
      <label>iFlow ID</label>
      <input type="text" id="iflowInput" placeholder="Enter iFlow artifact ID" />
    </div>
    <button class="btn" id="scanBtn" onclick="runScan()">Scan Resources</button>
  </div>
  <div id="resultsContainer"></div>
</main>
<script>
async function runScan() {
  var btn = document.getElementById('scanBtn');
  var container = document.getElementById('resultsContainer');
  btn.disabled = true;
  btn.textContent = 'Scanning...';
  
  try {
    var response = await fetch(window.location.href, {
      method: 'POST',
      headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
      body: JSON.stringify({
        tenant: document.getElementById('tenantSelect').value,
        iFlowID: document.getElementById('iflowInput').value
      })
    });
    var data = await response.json();
    container.innerHTML = renderResults(data);
    container.classList.add('active');
  } catch (err) {
    container.innerHTML = '<div style="color:red;padding:20px">Error: ' + err.message + '</div>';
    container.classList.add('active');
  }
  btn.disabled = false;
  btn.textContent = 'Scan Resources';
}

function renderResults(data) {
  var s = data.summary;
  return '<div class="summary-grid">' +
    renderCard('Groovy Scripts', s.total.groovy, s.used.groovy, s.unused.groovy) +
    renderCard('Message Mappings', s.total.mmap, s.used.mmap, s.unused.mmap) +
    renderCard('XSLT Mappings', s.total.xslt, s.used.xslt, s.unused.xslt) +
    renderCard('XSD Schemas', s.total.xsd, s.used.xsd, s.unused.xsd) +
  '</div>' +
  '<div class="panels">' +
    renderPanel('All Resources', data.all, '') +
    renderPanel('Used Resources', data.used.direct, '') +
    renderPanel('Unused Resources', data.unused, 'unused') +
  '</div>';
}

function renderCard(title, total, used, unused) {
  var cls = unused > 0 ? 'metric-card unused' : 'metric-card';
  return '<div class="' + cls + '">' +
    '<div class="metric-title">' + title + '</div>' +
    '<div style="display:flex;gap:20px">' +
      '<div><div class="metric-value">' + total + '</div><div class="metric-label">Total</div></div>' +
      '<div><div class="metric-value">' + used + '</div><div class="metric-label">Used</div></div>' +
      '<div><div class="metric-value">' + unused + '</div><div class="metric-label">Unused</div></div>' +
    '</div></div>';
}

function renderPanel(title, resources, itemClass) {
  var html = '<div class="panel"><div class="panel-header">' + title + '</div><div class="panel-body">';
  ['groovy', 'mmap', 'xslt', 'xsd'].forEach(function(type) {
    var list = resources[type] || [];
    list.forEach(function(name) {
      html += '<div class="resource-item ' + itemClass + '">' + name + '</div>';
    });
  });
  return html + '</div></div>';
}
</script>
</body>
</html>'''
}

4.  What the Dashboard Shows

Full WebUI showing summary metric cards and three-panel result layout

The interface shows:

  • Summary cards with Total / Used / Unused counts per file type
  • Three panels: All Resources, Used Resources, Unused Resources
  • Which XSD schemas and Groovy UDFs are hidden inside each Message Mapping
  • Excel export for documentation

Lessons Learned

While building this tool I encountered a few edge cases which are worth knowing about:

Global Script Bundles. An iFlow can reference Groovy scripts from external Script Collections via the scriptBundleID property. These groovy scripts are not part of the iFlow's own resources folder - they live in a separate, shared package. The scanner will show them as "used" (because they appear in the canvas reference), but they will not appear in the "all" list (because they are not in this iFlow's resources). This is expected behavior, not a bug. If you see a script in "used" that is missing from "all", check whether it comes from a Script Collection.

def isGlobalTarget = parent.children().findAll { it.name() == 'property' }.any { siblingProp ->
    def siblingKey = siblingProp.'**'.find { it.name() == 'key' }?.text()?.trim()
    def siblingValue = siblingProp.'**'.find { it.name() == 'value' }?.text()?.trim()
    
    // Check if the key matches and the value is not null or empty string
    return siblingKey == 'scriptBundleId' && siblingValue
}

Phantom References. Sometimes a Message Mapping internally references a Groovy UDF or XSD that no longer exists in the resources folder. The file may have been deleted, or the mapping was copied from another iFlow without its dependencies. In these cases, Total will not equal Used + Unused - there is a reference to something that is not there. The scanner exposes this inconsistency, which is actually useful: it flags mappings that may fail at runtime.

Commas in Filenames. The scanner stores resource lists as comma-separated strings in exchange properties. If a filename contains a comma (e.g., "Script,Helper,Utils.groovy"), the split logic will treat it as three separate files. This is a delimiter collision. The fix is to avoid commas in filenames - this is good practice anyway since commas are a common delimiter across many tools and systems.

The scanner finds static references - files assigned to steps or declared inside mappings. If a Groovy script constructs a filename at runtime and loads it dynamically, the scanner will not catch that. This is uncommon but worth checking before deleting anything flagged as unused.

Key Takeaways

After reading this post, you should be able to:

  • Serve a browser UI directly from CPI: No external hosting needed. Set Content-Type: text/html and return HTML content from Groovy.
  • Access iFlow internals via OData APIs: The /Resources endpoint gives the full catalog; the ZIP download gives the .iflw canvas file and .mmap mapping files.
  • Detect hidden dependencies: Message Mappings reference XSD schemas and Groovy UDFs internally via lnkRole elements. These are invisible on the canvas but can be extracted by parsing the .mmap XML.
  • Interpret count mismatches: When Total ≠ Used + Unused, it means either an external Script Bundle is in use, or a mapping references a file that no longer exists.
  • Apply this pattern to other tools: The HTML-from-Groovy pattern and the ProcessDirect-based engine separation work for any CPI utility that needs a browser interface.

The JSON output gives a clean list of only the active files. This is useful beyond just cleanup. For example, sending iFlow code to an LLM for documentation works better when you send only the files that are actually in use instead of the entire resource folder. That approach will be covered in the next post.

How do you keep your iFlows clean? Questions, other approaches or edge cases welcome in the comments.

AI Disclosure: AI was used to help structure and refine the language in this post. The technical content, code, and lessons learned are from my own development work.

entry.title: Why data fragmentation limits AI in SAP landscapes
entry.content:
Almost every conversation these days related to SAP contains some reference to AI. This doesn't surprise me, as I don't know any person today that it's not acquainted with AI somehow. Even those whose knowledge on technology is very little have become accostumed to it.

When it comes to enterprise technology, AI has already proven capabilities, along with the validation of real-world implementations. Models being created today can predict outcomes, automate processes, and produce insights like never before — we personally have performed multiple trials in a controlled environment that provided us with very successful outcomes. However, the problems begin to occur when you step outside of these controlled environments and try to integrate AI into how businesses truly function, because generally speaking, there is an issue unrelated to the actual model. I think things would be easier if the following issue was understood: almost all problems that come with the use of AI, even in enterprise technology, are entirely human.

The most prevalent issue we meet is with the current state of the data on which those models are built. The SAP landscape has not been built in a straightforward, linear manner. SAP environments develop over time, sometimes decades, and they accumulate all types of complexity from different systems, customizations, and integrations. What you actually find when trying to integrate AI into a business is not one version of a “true” set of data; it’s numerous versions of that same set of data existing simultaneously. This is where the difficulty in the implementation of AI begins.

From our observations, typically when implemented in a targeted manner, AI can quickly demonstrate its intended utility. You understand what it is you want to do, prepare your data and create your model, then produce relevant results. That’s not the problem. The contingency occurs immediately after implementation when there is momentum to take that across the enterprise as part of normal operations. At the time of enterprise rollout, the model is no longer operating against a pre-qualified dataset, but is functioning against the full complexity of the SAP ecosystem. Data is sourced from multiple places, using different formats, at different frequencies and sometimes even with differing definitions of the same element. The model continues to perform in its intended manner, but at this point, it is performing on a fragmented foundation.

This scenario was demonstrated to us on a recent project in the energy market where our objective was to forecast energy demand and supply as well as price by aggregating historical production and operational data with meteorological variables. The end product was a neural network model that has been trained with 3+ years of historical data, and the results will be made available through a dashboard to provide users with expected outcomes in order to support their decision-making process.

When viewed through the lens of modeling, everything operated as expected. The model predictions aligned with the actual results and provided analytical evidence to support previously undiscovered trends. However, the challenge was not necessarily the model itself, but rather the data that it was being provided either directly or indirectly to derive these insights.

We aggregated a multitude of data sources including electricity data, weather patterns, and pricing data to make predictions and develop forecasts. Each data source had its own unique structure, timing and granularity. Therefore, there were occasions where small misalignment between these disparate data sources would result in inconsistent predicted results.

This is where the most significant issues began to emerge, as various teams were relying on a different set of datasets when the model generated a forecast, which led to a disconnect from one team to the next. As such, teams would validate the model output against their own internal generated data, which would then lead to the model output being challenged instead of acted upon. While the insights were available, confidence in the insights was not. We’ve seen this pattern countless times, where fragmented data doesn’t stop AI from generating insights, but it does stop people from trusting and acting on them.

What we've learned is that scaling AI in SAP environments is actually a lot more about fixing the underlying data than about building a better AI model. Once you get your data to be more consistent and integrated and shared more easily across systems, everything else becomes a lot easier to do and move along at a faster pace.

We have experienced firsthand how a technically safe solution can have trouble because of data alignment issues, and we have seen how fairly simple models can actually generate significant value as long as your data foundation is solid. So the difference between these two situations is not the AI itself, but rather the environment in which the AI is being used.

Ultimately, the only time AI will matter is when it can lead to actual business decisions that take place. And in SAP landscapes, the transition from insight to action is very much dependent upon the connectivity and reliability of the data.

Therefore, while it may seem logical to focus on what your model can do for you, the more critical question for businesses is whether their data is ready to support scaling of AI. If it's not, scaling AI will always feel harder than it should be, no matter how good your models are.

entry.title: Joule AI Custom Solution for Material Shortage and Fulfillment
entry.content:
A few weeks ago, I had the opportunity to participate in a hackathon to build a Joule AI solution that addresses material shortages in an expedited manner. This can help Inventory Management users save time and money.

I am sure many SAP customers have similar requirements, and I want to share my experience here so it can be beneficial to others.

Problem Statement: IM users have to manually check shortages in SAP applications and create purchase requisitions if there is any shortage, to make sure production runs smoothly. Users perform this activity in the backend S/4HANA system.

Solution Architecture & Design: I built the artifacts below as part of this Joule project to address the requirement.

  1. Actions to get material shortages and create purchase requisitions.
  2. Skills to call these APIs and perform tasks.
  3. An agent that can process user requests.
  4. A test environment to deploy and test the project.

A destination to the backend system already exists to leverage the APIs and to create and test actions.

Actions built in SAP Build:

The action below was created to read material shortages from the backend by leveraging the Material Coverage API.

I also created a material input variable to filter the API using the material number. You can adjust the input and output as needed and test them in this section.

A condition is applied to map the user input material to the API material attribute in the C_MaterialCoverage entity.

Like the Material Coverage API, I configured another action to create a purchase requisition in the backend system. Please remember to enable CSRF protection (if applicable) for that POST API call.

Now let’s go into the Joule Studio to build the Joule project. As you can see from the screenshot below, a couple of Joule skills and an agent have been created.

This Joule skill is responsible for creating purchase requisition in the backend system.

Joule skills can also be consumed directly without an agent. You can enable or disable the functionality, as highlighted below.

These are the minimum inputs required to create a purchase requisition. Of course, it depends on how the backend is configured for a particular document type.

The purchase requisition number is the output from the skill.

Everything we’ve seen in the screenshots above is related to the Trigger part.

Below is the step where the corresponding Purchase Requisition action has been configured. A backend destination variable is also configured to create the purchase requisition in the corresponding backend system.

There are multiple other options available to use as well, like Process, Condition, etc.

The Trigger inputs are mapped to the Action inputs.

This is the output from the Action.

At the end the Action result is mapped to the Purchase Requisition variable which we are interested in.

Like the Purchase requisition skill, Material Shortage skill is also configured.

Now it’s time to configure the agent.

It’s important to set the expertise and instructions so the agent understands how to behave.

There are multiple LLM providers available in the Model Settings: OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and Mistral AI.

You can select any provider and the LLM they offer for response generation and planning.

Once everything is done, release and deploy the application.

A new test environment has been created under Control Tower to deploy and test this project.

To launch the agent, navigate to the Joule tab and click Launch.

It’s testing time.

I entered a prompt to pull the shortage information for a product. As you can see in the screenshot below, the agent responded with the shortage details. This information is pulled from the backend system.

Next, I want to create a purchase requisition. The expectation was that the agent would reuse as much information as possible from the previous step and ask questions only if additional input was required.

As expected, the agent understood the request and figured out that some inputs were still missing to create the purchase requisition. It asked for those details to proceed.

Here you go. The requisition has been created in the backend.

You can check the log by clicking the highlighted button top right corner.

Conclusion: The Joule AI solution is flexible enough to understand user requests and process them. Users don't have to navigate through multiple screens to complete the job.

AI solutions are powerful because they make many processes simple and easy. They can support different scenarios depending on how the agent is configured. There are J4D, J4C, and other tools that can help developers, consultants, and businesses save a lot of time and add value.

Key Challenges: I found that date mapping is not easy in the formula editor, since the skill inputs are always strings.

Error handling is also challenging. The skill doesn’t provide a way to map action/API errors so the agent can guide the user to resolve the issue in the back end. For example: The Purchasing Organization doesn’t exist or authorization errors.

SAP Joule Studio is evolving, and I hope these gaps will be addressed soon.

Please feel free to comment if you have any questions. l will try to answer as much as I can.

Thank you!

entry.title: Agentic AI and the Future of Finance
entry.content:
SAP's finance applications have transformed from traditional ERP systems into intelligent, cloud-native platforms that deliver real-time insights and strategic value to modern enterprises.  The evolution reflects a fundamental transition from transactional back-office processing to strategic business enablement, driven by CFOs' demand for real-time visibility, predictive analytics, and AI driven automation.

Finance departments have benefited from AI and Automation for years.  The earliest example is rules-based systems supported by analytics.  These systems were used for credit scoring, accounting reconciliation and fraud detection.  They basically relied on “if-then” logic to enforce static parameters.

The introduction of statistical modeling and machine learning provided more intelligence by incorporating forecasting and other predictive analytic algorithms to determine the probability or propensity of an outcome.  This helped tremendously to identify what new types of behaviors could indicate fraud.

Natural language processing is the foundation for generative AI.  By connecting to Large Language Models (LLMs), this capability enables you to have a conversation with your data.  This helps to describe what is possibly making new activity fraudulent or identify root causes of anomalies in your financial analysis. Based on the outcomes, semi-autonomous agents can execute the appropriate actions. Based on the results, semi-autonomous agents can execute the appropriate actions. 

Evolution of AI and Automation

This brings us to the holy grail – human managed agent-driven autonomous enterprise.

Future Vision of AI and Automation

 All the previous stages do not disappear. The evolve or continue but will eventually be managed by the agents.   This encourages you to get started now to get the incremental benefits of the individual stages.Sample Benefits

 As you can see in the samples, financial benefit has increased over time.  One of the biggest factors impacting the value realized from AI is the use case.  It is great to have experiments in labs to familiarize your teams with the technology, but the real impact comes from applying Agentic AI to core enterprise processes.

Example of Inter-departmental Processes

 

We have all heard that data is the new oil.  Just like your car needs the right viscosity. AI needs the right data. Connecting your systems requires more than just a wire.  Whether it is a human, an automated process, or an agent, there must be clean, real-time data. The APIs must be understandable and support consistent business semantics from all perspectives. 

If you don’t know where you are going any road will take you there. Financial Planning and Analysis (FP&A) provides the baseline of where your company is now and the guidance to align teams for success.

Analysis of current processes will determine if they are aligned to the objectives identified in the financial plan. Automation of the optimized processes will enhance efficiencies.  Embedded AI and custom agents will take automated processes to the next level.

One of the key components of effective AI is business context.  Having the proper semantics for agents and skills to communicate effectively is paramount to success.

And last but certainly not least is AI Governance. Robust AI Governance provides the visibility and control of process execution. 

Platform Capabilities for your Autonomous Journey

 

Integration SuiteFinancial Planning & AnalysisProcess MiningJoule StudioCustom AIBusiness Data CloudAI Governance

To learn more please visit the links or come hear Accenture speak at Sapphire about their journey to the intelligent enterprise.

Sapphire Orlando 2026

entry.title: The Flyweel is turning: Partners leverage SAP Business Data Cloud and SAP BTP during Hack2Build
entry.content:
In the fast-paced digital landscape of 2025, SAP unveiled its plans for SAP Business Data Cloud as well as SAP HANA Cloud at SAP TechEd 2025, positioning itself as a leading force in data-driven innovation. Building on these announcements, SAP’s latest Hack2Build event explored the full potential of building applications on SAP Business Data Cloud.

The Hack2Build on Building Applications on SAP Business Data Cloud provided an immersive environment for partners to leverage the advanced capabilities of SAP Business Data Cloud as well as HANA Cloud and BTP, thus tapping even into the realm of agentic AI.  Equipped with hands-on coaching from SAP experts, participating partners became frontrunners in leveraging cutting-edge technologies, empowering them to transform insights into actionable solutions.

During the Hack2Build initiative, partners engaged in a seven-day hack phase, constructing functional applications that harnessed the power of real-time data analytics, advanced AI-driven business intelligence and actionable insights. The event emphasized seamless integration with SAP and non-SAP systems, and the capability to translate raw data into analytical insights and from there into strategic actions.

The culmination of the event featured a Live Demo Jam, where partners presented their innovative prototypes to a panel of SAP judges. This collaborative event showcased the ingenuity and potential of SAP's latest technology advancements, paving the way for future applications that bridge the gap between data insights and actionable business strategies.

It was again amazing to see how partners turned their innovative solution visions into running prototypes within seven days and delivering even full breakthroughs from sourcing the data through analytical modeling into leveraging the insights in Joule agents. The flywheel is truly turning now!

Participating Partners:

Participating Partners in the Hack2Build Building Applications on BDC, Apr 22-29 2026

🏆Winners

With that momentum in motion, the judges selected three solutions that stood out across technical completeness, workflow clarity, and customer relevance.

🥇NTT Data Business Solutions 

Manufacturers often face unexpected disruptions that reduce operational efficiency and can delay customer deliveries. The Intelligent Manufacturing solution addresses this by using SAP Business Data Cloud and SAP Business Technology Platform to combine data from SAP ERP, SAP Data Products, custom Data Products, machine telemetry, and energy consumption. These data sources are used to train AI models in SAP Databricks, enabling early detection of production orders at risk due to supplier delays, plant failures, equipment issues, lack of resources, or other factors. The solution provides actionable insights through SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Build, while SAP Build Process Automation triggers real-time notifications for risk mitigation.

 

🥈cbs Consulting

cbs solution AI-Driven Dynamic Safety Stock Optimization aims at transforming supply chain resilience using SAP BDC, BTP, and AI. By integrating SAP S/4HANA and SAP IBP data with external market drivers (Oil prices, Forex Rates, & Trade Routes) via SAP Datasphere, the data is harmonized, while predictive models from SAP Databricks assess demand variability, supply risk, and external factors to generate optimal stock recommendations. SAP Analytics Cloud provides simulations, insights, and planning capabilities, while SAP Build Apps triggers the multi-level approval workflow and operationalise decisions back into core systems (SAP S/4HANA). The result? A closed-loop, intelligent process that reduces excess inventory and boosts market responsiveness.

 

🥉SDG Group

In oil and gas, an unplanned equipment failure costs $45,000–$60,000 per hour. The manual response cycle stretches that loss across 48 hours on average. Rig-Guard consolidates historical NPT records and live sensor readings (temperature, pressure, vibration) into a single source of truth on SAP BDC, then runs a predictive ML model on SAP Databricks to catch failures before they happen. When a prediction fires, an agentic workflow on SAP Build Process Automation takes it from insight to action: Autonomous above 95% confidence, Human in the Loop below - failure caught, revenue protected. The rig watches itself. The rig never stops.

 

⭐ Finalists

All other finalists proved themselves to be winners in their own right through the enthusiasm and creativity they demonstrated, underscoring the vitality of SAP's partner ecosystem. Across industries such as finance, supply chain, retail, and more, these accomplished partners delivered BDC-based solutions with clear business relevance and strong potential for rapid customer adoption. Their impressive prototypes showcased how Applications, Data & AI come to life and truly turn the flywheel.

 

Partner Company

Solution Title

All for One

AI Planning Insight Agent for SAP Analytics Cloud & Datasphere

cbs

AI-Driven Dynamic Safety Stock Optimization

Deloitte

CFO Impact Analyzer

DXC

SAP Customer Delivery Preference Identification

EY

MarketIQ

IBM

Revenue Timing & Value Conflicts with Tariff Resolution

Infosys

Infosys Size Curve Prediction Solution for Retail

KPMG

AI Copilot for Real-Time Supplier Risk & Sourcing Decisions

NTT DATA Business Solutions

Manufacturing Intelligence

PWC

24/7 Reconciliation & Monitoring

 

What’s Next

All participating partners now move into a six‑week Build Phase, where they will refine and extend their prototypes with support from SAP product management, Partner Ecosystem Success as well as many other experts in BDC, BTP and AI. This phase will focus on preparing solutions for customer validation and potential go‑to‑market discussions.

Acknowledgments

Many thanks to all partner teams, Judges, Databricks team and SAP contributors across Business Data Cloud Product Management, Customer Success & Delivery, Value Prototyping, BTP, BDC and AI Solution Architects, Partner Excellence Center Leads and Partner Ecosystem Success for making this edition of Hack2Build a milestone in spinning the flywheel of Applications, Data & AI!
